exoplanet.name: Exoplanet
exoplanet.description: A planet located outside the Solar System, orbiting another star. Imagine the Sun as a solitary lamp in a room, and exoplanets as fireflies circling other lamps in a vast hall, which we barely notice due to the brightness of the lamps themselves.
exoplanet.history: The first confirmed discovery of an exoplanet around a Sun-like star was made in 1995 by Michel Mayor and Didier Queloz, for which they received the Nobel Prize in 2019. Earlier, in 1992, planets were found around a pulsar, but that was a special case. Now over 5,000 exoplanets have been discovered, and detection methods are constantly improving.
exoplanet.how_it_works: Exoplanets are detected mainly by two methods: the transit method (the planet passes in front of the star, slightly dimming its brightness) and the radial velocity method (the planet's gravity causes the star to wobble slightly). Analogy: imagine trying to notice a fly flying in front of a bright spotlight — the fly is barely visible, but the light flickers slightly.
key_problems: How to accurately determine the composition of an exoplanet's atmosphere and the presence of biomarkers such as oxygen or methane?
Why do many exoplanets have orbits unlike those in the Solar System (e.g., hot Jupiters)?
exoplanet.fun_fact: Some exoplanets are likely covered in oceans of lava or diamonds, and on one (HD 189733b) it rains molten glass, flying sideways due to hurricane-force winds.

formulas.meaning: The square of the planet's orbital period is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis of its orbit.

black_hole.name: Black Hole
black_hole.description: A region of spacetime where gravity is so strong that nothing, not even light, can escape. Imagine a whirlpool in the ocean that sucks everything around it — only instead of water, it's curved space, and instead of a funnel, it's the event horizon.
black_hole.history: The idea of objects with such gravity appeared as early as 1783 with John Michell and later with Laplace. In 1915, Karl Schwarzschild found an exact solution to Einstein's equations describing a black hole. In the 1960s, John Wheeler popularized the term 'black hole'. In 2019, the Event Horizon Telescope obtained the first image of a black hole's shadow in the galaxy M87.
black_hole.how_it_works: When a massive star exhausts its nuclear fuel, its core collapses under its own gravity, forming a black hole. Gravity is so strong that space and time swap roles: inside the event horizon, the singularity is not a point in space but a moment in the future. Analogy: if you fall into a black hole, to an outside observer you will slow down and redden, while you yourself will see the entire history of the universe flash before you.
key_problems: What happens to information that falls into a black hole? This is known as the information paradox, which challenges quantum mechanics.
What does the interior of a black hole look like, and does the singularity actually exist?
black_hole.fun_fact: If the Sun became a black hole of the same radius, its gravity at Earth's orbit would not change — we would simply freeze in the dark.

formulas.meaning: The radius of a sphere from which even light cannot escape.

neutron_star.name: Neutron Star
neutron_star.description: An extremely dense remnant of a massive star after a supernova explosion, composed mainly of neutrons. Imagine compressing the entire population of Earth into the volume of a sugar cube — that's how dense a neutron star is.
neutron_star.history: The existence of neutron stars was predicted in 1934 by Walter Baade and Fritz Zwicky, shortly after the discovery of the neutron. In 1967, Jocelyn Bell Burnell discovered pulsars — rapidly rotating neutron stars emitting radio pulses. This discovery confirmed their existence and earned her supervisor a Nobel Prize.
neutron_star.how_it_works: When a star with a mass between 8 and 30 solar masses explodes as a supernova, its core collapses, and protons and electrons merge into neutrons. Gravity compresses matter to nuclear density, and the neutron star stabilizes due to degenerate neutron pressure. Analogy: it's like trying to compress a soccer ball to the size of a grain of sand — it would become incredibly hard and dense.
key_problems: What is the equation of state of neutron matter? We don't know exactly how neutrons behave at such densities.
What is at the center of a neutron star — possibly quark-gluon plasma?
neutron_star.fun_fact: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons — roughly the weight of Mount Everest.

formulas.meaning: The maximum mass of a white dwarf; beyond it, it collapses into a neutron star.

galaxy.name: Galaxy
galaxy.description: A giant gravitationally bound system of stars, gas, dust, and dark matter. Our galaxy, the Milky Way, contains hundreds of billions of stars. Imagine a city of stars, where each star is a house, and dark matter is the invisible foundation holding everything together.
galaxy.history: In ancient times, the Milky Way was considered a milky road of gods. In the 18th century, William Herschel made the first map of our galaxy. In the 1920s, Edwin Hubble proved that the Andromeda Nebula is a separate galaxy, not a nebula within the Milky Way, expanding the boundaries of the universe.
galaxy.how_it_works: Galaxies form from giant clouds of gas and dark matter that collapse under gravity. Stars are born inside galaxies, and their motion obeys the laws of gravity. However, visible mass does not explain the rotation speed of stars at the outskirts — dark matter is needed. Analogy: a galaxy is like a whirlpool, where stars are chips, and dark matter is the water, invisible but creating the current.
key_problems: What is dark matter, which makes up most of the mass of galaxies?
How do supermassive black holes at the centers of galaxies affect their evolution?
galaxy.fun_fact: At the center of our galaxy is a supermassive black hole, Sagittarius A*, with a mass of about 4 million solar masses, but it is very quiet compared to others.

quasar.name: Quasar
quasar.description: An extremely bright active galactic nucleus powered by a supermassive black hole. Quasars are the brightest objects in the universe, outshining entire galaxies. Imagine a spotlight with the power of a trillion suns shining from the center of a distant city.
quasar.history: The first quasars were discovered in the 1960s as star-like radio sources. In 1963, Maarten Schmidt determined that the spectrum of quasar 3C 273 had a huge redshift, indicating its colossal distance. This was a sensation: such bright objects must be incredibly powerful.
quasar.how_it_works: A supermassive black hole at the center of a galaxy attracts gas and dust, forming an accretion disk. As matter falls in, it accelerates to near-light speeds, friction heats it to millions of degrees, and it emits enormous energy, including X-rays. Analogy: it's like a waterfall where water falls from a great height and splashes, creating bright sprays of light.
key_problems: How do quasars influence the formation and evolution of galaxies?
Why does quasar activity cease and they become ordinary galaxies?
quasar.fun_fact: Light from the brightest quasar 3C 273 takes 2.4 billion years to reach us, so we see it as it was when the first multicellular organisms appeared on Earth.

pulsar.name: Pulsar
pulsar.description: A rapidly rotating neutron star that emits narrow beams of radio radiation, which we perceive as periodic pulses. Imagine a lighthouse that rotates and sends out a beam of light — if you stand in its path, you see flashes at regular intervals.
pulsar.history: Pulsars were discovered in 1967 by Jocelyn Bell Burnell, then a graduate student, who noticed regular radio pulses. Initially they were jokingly called LGM-1 (Little Green Men), but soon it was understood they were natural objects. For this discovery, her supervisor Antony Hewish received the Nobel Prize.
pulsar.how_it_works: A neutron star rotates at tremendous speed (up to hundreds of revolutions per second) and has a strong magnetic field. Particles are accelerated along the magnetic poles, creating narrow beams of radiation. If the beam hits Earth, we see a pulse. Analogy: if you spin a flashlight, an observer sees flashes only when the beam is pointed at them.
key_problems: What is the internal structure of neutron stars?
Why do some pulsars have glitches — sudden increases in rotation speed?
pulsar.fun_fact: The fastest pulsar, PSR J1748-2446ad, rotates at 716 revolutions per second — faster than the blades of a kitchen blender.

supernova.name: Supernova
supernova.description: A powerful stellar explosion that can temporarily outshine an entire galaxy. This is the final stage of evolution for massive stars or a thermonuclear explosion of a white dwarf. Imagine a star as a balloon being inflated to the limit, then it bursts, scattering pieces in all directions.
supernova.history: Supernovae have been observed since ancient times: for example, the supernova of 1054 created the Crab Nebula. In the 1930s, Walter Baade and Fritz Zwicky first linked supernovae to the formation of neutron stars. In 1987, supernova SN 1987A exploded in the Large Magellanic Cloud — the first visible to the naked eye in 400 years.
supernova.how_it_works: For massive stars: when the core runs out of fuel, pressure drops, and the core collapses, forming a neutron star or black hole, while the outer layers are ejected in an explosion. For white dwarfs: accretion of matter from a companion star exceeds the Chandrasekhar limit, triggering a thermonuclear explosion. Analogy: it's like the lower floors of a skyscraper collapsing, causing the entire building to fold inward and then explode.
key_problems: How to accurately predict which stars will become supernovae?
What is the explosion mechanism of Type II supernovae — neutrino heating or something else?
supernova.fun_fact: Supernovae are the main forges of heavy elements: gold, silver, and uranium are born in these explosions, so your jewelry is literally stardust.

comet.name: Comet
comet.description: A small icy body in the Solar System that, when approaching the Sun, forms a tail of gas and dust. Comets are 'dirty snowballs' of ice, dust, and organics. Imagine taking a piece of ice out into the sun — it will start to melt and evaporate, forming a cloud of vapor.
comet.history: Comets have been known since antiquity, often seen as omens of disaster. Edmund Halley in 1705 predicted the return of a comet (now Halley's Comet) based on his calculations. In 1950, Jan Oort proposed the existence of a comet cloud at the edge of the Solar System (the Oort Cloud). The Rosetta mission in 2014 landed a probe on comet 67P/Churyumov-Gerasimenko for the first time.
comet.how_it_works: When a comet approaches the Sun, ice sublimates, ejecting gas and dust. The solar wind and light pressure form two tails: a gas (ion) tail pointing away from the Sun, and a dust tail curved along the orbit. Analogy: it's like a snowball you throw into a fire — it hisses, emits steam, and leaves a trail.
key_problems: What is the exact composition of comets and how does it relate to the composition of the early Solar System?
Can comets deliver organics and water to Earth?
comet.fun_fact: Halley's Comet returns every 75-76 years, and in 2061 it will be visible to the naked eye again.

asteroid.name: Asteroid
asteroid.description: A small rocky or metallic body orbiting the Sun, usually in the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter. Asteroids are the building remnants from which planets formed. Imagine rocks of various sizes left over after building a house.
asteroid.history: The first asteroid, Ceres, was discovered in 1801 by Giuseppe Piazzi. Initially considered a planet, but later many similar objects were found. In the 1990s, missions to asteroids began: NEAR Shoemaker landed on Eros, and Japan's Hayabusa mission returned samples from Itokawa.
asteroid.how_it_works: Asteroids move in orbits around the Sun, their sizes range from a few meters to hundreds of kilometers. They can collide with each other, forming smaller fragments. Some asteroids have their own moons. Analogy: it's like rocks in a washing machine drum — they collide, break apart, and sometimes fly out.
key_problems: How to protect Earth from potentially hazardous asteroids?
What is the composition of asteroids and how does it change with distance from the Sun?
asteroid.fun_fact: The asteroid Bennu, studied by the OSIRIS-REx mission, contains organic molecules and water, supporting the theory that water was delivered to Earth by asteroids.

red_dwarf.name: Red Dwarf
red_dwarf.description: The most common type of star in the galaxy: small, cool, and dim, but incredibly long-lived. Red dwarfs live for trillions of years — longer than the current age of the universe. Imagine a glowing ember that will burn almost forever, unlike a bright bonfire.
red_dwarf.history: Red dwarfs were first classified in the early 20th century by spectral types. For a long time, it was thought they could not have planets due to low luminosity, but the discovery of exoplanets around the red dwarf Gliese 581 in 2007 changed that view. Now red dwarfs are a prime target in the search for habitable worlds.
red_dwarf.how_it_works: Red dwarfs have masses from 0.08 to 0.5 solar masses. In their interiors, a slow thermonuclear reaction occurs, and convection fully mixes the material, allowing all fuel to be used. Due to low temperature, they emit mainly in the red and infrared range. Analogy: it's like a candle that burns very dimly but lasts for thousands of years.
key_problems: Can planets in the habitable zone of red dwarfs be suitable for life, given flares and tidal locking?
How do red dwarfs form and why are they so numerous?
red_dwarf.fun_fact: The closest star to the Sun, Proxima Centauri, is a red dwarf, and it has an Earth-like planet in the habitable zone.

white_dwarf.name: White dwarf
white_dwarf.description: A dense remnant of a star like the Sun after it has shed its outer layers. A white dwarf consists of degenerate electron gas and gradually cools over billions of years. Imagine a cooling ember in a fire — it no longer burns but remains hot for a long time.
white_dwarf.history: The first discovered white dwarf was Sirius B in 1862. In 1926, Ralph Fowler explained its structure using quantum mechanics and the Pauli principle. Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar in 1930 calculated the maximum mass of a white dwarf (the Chandrasekhar limit), for which he received the Nobel Prize.
white_dwarf.how_it_works: When a Sun-like star exhausts its nuclear fuel, it sheds its outer layers, forming a planetary nebula, and the core collapses into a white dwarf. The pressure of degenerate electrons prevents further collapse. The white dwarf is initially very hot but gradually cools, becoming a black dwarf. Analogy: it is like ash after a fire — still hot but no longer burning.
key_problems: What is the cooling rate of white dwarfs and what does it tell us about the age of the universe?
What happens when a white dwarf exceeds the Chandrasekhar limit (e.g., by accreting matter)?
white_dwarf.fun_fact: A diamond is crystalline carbon, and white dwarfs often consist of carbon and oxygen, so they can be considered giant Earth-sized diamonds.

formulas.meaning: The maximum mass of a white dwarf; beyond it, the star collapses into a neutron star.

sun.name: Sun
sun.description: The star at the center of the Solar System, the source of light and heat for Earth. The Sun is a yellow dwarf, composed mainly of hydrogen and helium. Imagine a giant thermonuclear reactor that has been operating for 4.6 billion years and will continue for another 4.6 billion.
sun.history: The Sun has been revered since antiquity as a deity. In the 16th century, Copernicus placed the Sun at the center of the universe. In the 1920s, Arthur Eddington suggested that the Sun's energy source is thermonuclear fusion. In 1938, Hans Bethe described the proton-proton chain in detail, for which he received the Nobel Prize.
sun.how_it_works: In the Sun's core, at a temperature of 15 million degrees, hydrogen is converted into helium through thermonuclear reactions, releasing energy. This energy gradually makes its way to the surface, radiating as light and heat. Solar activity, including sunspots and flares, is related to the magnetic field. Analogy: the Sun is like a huge furnace where hydrogen burns, and we receive heat and light.
key_problems: Why is the Sun's corona hotter than its surface (millions of degrees vs. 5500°C)?
How to predict solar flares and their impact on Earth?
sun.fun_fact: Every second, the Sun converts 4 million tons of matter into energy, but its mass remains practically unchanged.

formulas.meaning: The total power output of the Sun.

nebula.name: Nebula
nebula.description: A giant cloud of gas and dust in space where stars are born or which remains after their death. Nebulae can be emission, reflection, or dark. Imagine a cosmic watercolor painting: bright colors are glowing gas, and dark patches are dust clouds.
nebula.history: The term 'nebula' was used for any fuzzy object in the sky until the 1920s when Edwin Hubble proved that many are separate galaxies. In the 18th century, Charles Messier compiled a catalog of nebulae to avoid confusing them with comets. Modern telescopes like Hubble have revealed the incredible beauty of nebulae.
nebula.how_it_works: Emission nebulae glow because ionized gas (usually hydrogen) emits light upon recombination. Reflection nebulae scatter light from nearby stars. Dark nebulae are dense dust clouds that block light. Analogy: a nebula is like a cloud on Earth: some clouds glow from the sun, others are dark and rainy.
key_problems: How exactly does star formation occur in nebulae?
What is the role of magnetic fields in nebula dynamics?
nebula.fun_fact: The Orion Nebula is one of the brightest and closest star-forming regions, visible to the naked eye as a fuzzy patch in Orion's sword.

galaxy_cluster.name: Galaxy cluster
galaxy_cluster.description: A gravitationally bound group of hundreds or thousands of galaxies, permeated by hot gas and dark matter. These are among the most massive structures in the universe. Imagine a metropolis where each galaxy is a building, and the space between them is filled with invisible dark matter.
galaxy_cluster.history: The first galaxy clusters were discovered in the 18th century, but their nature became clear only in the 20th century. In 1933, Fritz Zwicky measured the velocities of galaxies in the Coma Cluster and found that visible mass was insufficient to hold them together — this was the first evidence of dark matter. Modern surveys like the Sloan Digital Sky Survey catalog thousands of clusters.
galaxy_cluster.how_it_works: Galaxy clusters are held together by gravity, with most of the mass in dark matter. Hot gas between galaxies emits X-rays, allowing clusters to be detected. Gravitational lensing bends light from distant galaxies, creating a clue. Analogy: a cluster is like a swarm of bees, where each bee is a galaxy, and the invisible hive is dark matter.
key_problems: What is the nature of dark matter that dominates clusters?
How do galaxy clusters form and evolve in the large-scale structure of the universe?
galaxy_cluster.fun_fact: The Bullet Cluster (1E 0657-558) provided direct evidence for dark matter: after two clusters collided, the gas remained in the center while dark matter passed through.

meteorite.name: Meteorite
meteorite.description: A solid body of cosmic origin that has fallen to the Earth's surface. Meteorites are fragments of asteroids, comets, or planets. Imagine a rock that came from space and landed in your yard — that is a meteorite.
meteorite.history: Meteorites have been known since antiquity, but their cosmic origin was proven only in 1803 after the fall of the L'Aigle meteorite in France. Ernst Chladni in 1794 suggested that stones fall from the sky. In the 20th century, analysis of meteorites provided clues to the composition of the Solar System and the age of Earth.
meteorite.how_it_works: Meteorites are remnants of meteoroids that did not burn up in the atmosphere and reached the surface. Upon entering the atmosphere, they heat up, forming a bright fireball. Meteorites are classified as stony, iron, and stony-iron. Analogy: it is like throwing a stone into water — it flies through the air (atmosphere) and sinks to the bottom.
key_problems: How often do large meteorite falls occur and what is the probability of a catastrophic impact?
Which meteorites contain organic molecules and could they indicate extraterrestrial life?
meteorite.fun_fact: The meteorite ALH84001, found in Antarctica, is believed to be from Mars and contains structures that some scientists interpret as fossilized bacteria.

exomoon.name: Exomoon
exomoon.description: A moon orbiting an exoplanet outside the Solar System. Exomoons could be potential sites for life, as they may receive energy from the planet and star. Imagine our Moon orbiting Earth, but Earth is around another star — that is an exomoon.
exomoon.history: The first exomoon candidates were discovered in 2018: Kepler-1625b I, found using transit data from the Kepler telescope. However, confirmation requires additional observations as the signal is weak. The search for exomoons is a new field of astronomy, actively developing with the launch of JWST.
exomoon.how_it_works: Exomoons are detected via the transit method: when a moon passes in front of its star, it causes an additional dip in brightness, and may also cause variations in the planet's transit timing. Analogy: it is like watching a streetlamp, with a fly (planet) flying in front, and then a mosquito (moon) — you notice a double flicker.
key_problems: How to reliably confirm the existence of an exomoon given the weakness of the signal?
Can exomoons be habitable, and what conditions are necessary?
exomoon.fun_fact: Some exomoons may be Earth-sized and have their own atmosphere, making them among the most interesting targets for the search for extraterrestrial life.

asteroid_belt.name: Asteroid belt
asteroid_belt.description: A region of the Solar System between the orbits of Mars and Jupiter, where most asteroids are concentrated. The belt contains millions of objects, from dust grains to dwarf planets. Imagine a ring of rocks orbiting the Sun between two planets.
asteroid_belt.history: The asteroid belt was discovered in the early 19th century when astronomers were searching for a 'missing' planet between Mars and Jupiter. The first discovered was Ceres (1801), followed by Pallas, Juno, and Vesta. By mid-century, it became clear that this was not a planet but a collection of bodies. Modern missions like Dawn have studied Vesta and Ceres.
asteroid_belt.how_it_works: Asteroids in the belt orbit the Sun; their total mass is small (about 4% of the Moon's mass). Jupiter's gravity prevents them from forming a planet, causing resonances and perturbations. Collisions fragment asteroids, creating dust and meteoroids. Analogy: it is like a construction site with many leftover stones, but the foreman (Jupiter) constantly interferes with their assembly.
key_problems: What is the history of the asteroid belt's formation and why did it not become a planet?
How do asteroids from the belt reach near-Earth orbits?
asteroid_belt.fun_fact: The dwarf planet Ceres, the largest object in the asteroid belt, contains more fresh water than all of Earth.

milky_way.name: Milky Way
milky_way.description: Our home galaxy, a huge stellar disk about 100,000 light-years across. Imagine a giant cosmic city where the Sun is one of billions of inhabitants, and we are on the outskirts, in one of the spiral arms.
milky_way.history: The ancient Greeks called it 'milk' because of the misty band in the sky. In 1610, Galileo discovered it was a multitude of stars. In the 1920s, Edwin Hubble proved that the Milky Way is just one of many galaxies.
milky_way.how_it_works: The Milky Way is a barred spiral galaxy rotating around its center, where a supermassive black hole resides. Stars move in orbits like planets around the Sun, but on a galactic scale.
key_problems: The exact structure and number of spiral arms are still being refined.
The nature of dark matter that holds the galaxy together.
milky_way.fun_fact: The Milky Way and the Andromeda Galaxy are moving toward each other and will collide in about 4 billion years.

brown_dwarf.name: Brown Dwarf
brown_dwarf.description: A celestial body occupying an intermediate position between a planet and a star. It is too massive to be a planet, but not massive enough to sustain hydrogen fusion. Like a teenager who hasn't grown into an adult but is no longer a child.
brown_dwarf.history: The existence of brown dwarfs was predicted by Shiv Kumar in the 1960s. The first confirmed brown dwarf, Teide 1, was discovered in 1995. Thousands are now known.
brown_dwarf.how_it_works: A brown dwarf forms from a gas and dust cloud like a star, but its mass is less than 0.08 solar masses. Deuterium fusion may occur in its interior, but not hydrogen fusion. It gradually cools and dims.
key_problems: The boundary between a brown dwarf and a giant planet is blurred.
Formation mechanisms: do they form like stars or like planets?
brown_dwarf.fun_fact: Brown dwarfs are not brown—they emit in the infrared and appear reddish.

spectroscopy.name: Spectroscopy
spectroscopy.description: A method of studying matter by its spectrum—splitting light into its constituent colors. Like fingerprints: each chemical element leaves a unique set of lines in the spectrum.
spectroscopy.history: Beginnings with Newton (splitting light with a prism) and Fraunhofer (dark lines in the Sun's spectrum). In the 19th century, Kirchhoff and Bunsen linked lines to elements. Spectroscopy is now the foundation of astrophysics.
spectroscopy.how_it_works: Light from a star passes through gas and is absorbed at specific wavelengths. The positions of lines reveal the composition, temperature, density, and motion of the object. The Doppler effect shifts lines, allowing velocity measurements.
key_problems: Accurate modeling of exoplanet atmosphere spectra.
Detection of biomarkers such as oxygen and methane.
spectroscopy.fun_fact: Using spectroscopy, astronomers can determine the composition of an exoplanet's atmosphere many light-years away.

formulas.meaning: The wavelength of maximum emission is inversely proportional to temperature

transit_method.name: Transit Method
transit_method.description: A method of detecting exoplanets by the periodic dimming of a star's light when a planet passes in front of its disk. Like a fly flying in front of a lamp: the light momentarily dims.
transit_method.history: First proposed in 1952 by Otto Struve. The first exoplanet discovered by this method (HD 209458b) was found in 1999. The transit method is now the primary method for finding exoplanets, especially thanks to the Kepler mission.
transit_method.how_it_works: If the planet's orbit is aligned so that it passes between the star and the observer, each transit causes a small drop in brightness. The depth and period of the transit reveal the planet's size and orbital period.
key_problems: False signals from binary stars and starspots.
Need for confirmation by other methods.
transit_method.fun_fact: The Kepler space telescope discovered over 2600 exoplanets using the transit method.

formulas.meaning: The brightness drop is proportional to the square of the ratio of the planet's radius to the star's radius

gravitational_lensing.name: Gravitational Lensing
gravitational_lensing.description: The bending of light from distant objects by the gravity of massive bodies. Like a glass lens, but gravity plays the role of the lens: it can magnify, distort, and even create multiple images.
gravitational_lensing.history: Predicted by Einstein's general theory of relativity in 1915. First observation: deflection of starlight during the 1919 solar eclipse. Strong lensing (Einstein rings) was first detected in 1979.
gravitational_lensing.how_it_works: A massive object (galaxy, cluster) warps spacetime. Light from a source behind it follows curved paths, creating a magnified or distorted image. The effect allows study of very distant and faint objects.
key_problems: Accurate reconstruction of mass distribution in the lens.
Use for measuring the Hubble constant.
gravitational_lensing.fun_fact: Gravitational lensing allows us to see galaxies that existed 500 million years after the Big Bang.

formulas.meaning: The deflection angle is proportional to mass and inversely proportional to the impact parameter b

photometry.name: Photometry
photometry.description: A method of measuring the intensity of light from astronomical objects. Like measuring the brightness of a light bulb, but for stars and galaxies. It allows determination of distances, sizes, and brightness changes.
photometry.history: Ancient astronomers estimated star brightness by eye (magnitudes). Photographic methods emerged in the 19th century. Modern photometry uses CCDs and precise filters.
photometry.how_it_works: Light is collected by a telescope and recorded by a detector. The number of photons is measured in different filters. Comparison with standard stars yields accurate brightness. Light curves are used to study variable stars and exoplanet transits.
key_problems: Accounting for atmospheric distortions in ground-based observations.
Calibration of photometric systems across different telescopes.
photometry.fun_fact: Photometry helped discover the first exoplanet transiting its star (HD 209458b).

formulas.meaning: The difference in magnitudes is related to the ratio of fluxes

radio_astronomy.name: Radio Astronomy
radio_astronomy.description: The study of cosmic objects through their radio emission. Like listening to the Universe instead of looking at it. Radio waves penetrate dust and gas, revealing what is invisible in the optical range.
radio_astronomy.history: Began in 1932 when Karl Jansky discovered radio emission from the Milky Way. Post-WWII radar development gave impetus. Giant radio telescopes like Arecibo and FAST were built.
radio_astronomy.how_it_works: Radio telescopes collect radio waves using antennas. The signal is amplified and processed. Radio astronomy allows study of pulsars, quasars, cosmic microwave background, molecular clouds, and active galactic nuclei.
key_problems: Radio interference from terrestrial sources.
High-resolution imaging requires interferometry.
radio_astronomy.fun_fact: Radio astronomy discovered the cosmic microwave background—the echo of the Big Bang.

collider.name: collider
collider.description: An accelerator where beams of particles collide at high energy. Like billiards, but the balls are elementary particles, and their collision allows us to peer into the microcosm and recreate the conditions of the early Universe.
collider.history: The first colliders appeared in the 1960s. The Large Hadron Collider (LHC) was launched in 2008. In 2012, the Higgs boson, predicted by Peter Higgs, was discovered there.
collider.how_it_works: Particles (e.g., protons) are accelerated in a ring accelerator to near-light speeds and collide. Detectors record the collision products. Data analysis allows studying fundamental interactions and searching for new particles.
key_problems: Search for dark matter particles.
Explanation of matter-antimatter asymmetry.
collider.fun_fact: The LHC is the largest and most complex machine in human history, with a ring length of 27 km.

formulas.meaning: In colliding beams, all energy goes into creating new particles

computer_simulation.name: computer simulation
computer_simulation.description: Creating mathematical models of physical processes and solving them numerically on a computer. Like a virtual laboratory where you can conduct an experiment impossible in reality—for example, a galaxy collision or the evolution of the Universe.
computer_simulation.history: The first computer calculations appeared in the 1950s. With the development of supercomputers, it became possible to simulate galaxy formation, climate, nuclear reactions. Simulations are now an integral part of science.
computer_simulation.how_it_works: Laws of physics are written as equations (hydrodynamics, gravity). Space and time are divided into cells, and the computer calculates the system's evolution step by step. Results are compared with observations.
key_problems: Limitations of computing power for accurate modeling.
Accounting for unknown processes, e.g., dark matter.
computer_simulation.fun_fact: Simulating galaxy formation requires calculating the motion of billions of particles under gravity.

interferometry.name: interferometry
interferometry.description: A method of combining signals from multiple telescopes to obtain high-resolution images. Like using several small mirrors to assemble one large one—only in radio and optical ranges.
interferometry.history: Invented in the late 19th century. First applied in astronomy to measure a star's diameter in 1920. Modern systems like the Event Horizon Telescope combine telescopes worldwide.
interferometry.how_it_works: Signals from telescopes are synchronized and combined. Phase differences allow reconstructing an image with resolution equivalent to a telescope the size of the baseline. The larger the baseline, the higher the resolution.
key_problems: Difficulty of synchronizing signals with high precision.
Atmospheric interference for optical interferometry.
interferometry.fun_fact: The Event Horizon Telescope, using interferometry, obtained the first image of a black hole in 2019.

jwst.name: JWST
jwst.description: The James Webb Space Telescope—the most powerful infrared telescope, launched in 2021. Like a time machine, allowing us to look back at the era of the first stars and galaxies.
jwst.history: Development began in 1996, launch was repeatedly delayed. After successful launch and deployment in 2022, JWST began scientific observations. It is the successor to Hubble.
jwst.how_it_works: JWST observes in the infrared range, allowing it to see through dust and study very distant objects. Its 6.5-meter mirror consists of 18 segments. The telescope is located at the L2 Lagrange point.
key_problems: Limited lifetime due to fuel reserves.
Complexity of calibration and data processing.
jwst.fun_fact: JWST is so sensitive it could see a bee on the Moon from Earth.

hubble.name: Hubble
hubble.description: The Hubble Space Telescope—a legendary observatory operating since 1990. Like a window to the Universe, free from atmospheric distortions, giving us the most beautiful and deepest images of space.
hubble.history: Launched in 1990, but due to a mirror defect, the first images were blurry. In 1993, a shuttle mission corrected the optics. Hubble has operated for over 30 years, making many discoveries.
hubble.how_it_works: The telescope with a 2.4-meter mirror collects light in optical and ultraviolet ranges. It is in low Earth orbit. It measured the acceleration of the Universe's expansion, discovered exoplanets, and obtained deep fields.
key_problems: Gradual degradation of equipment.
Need for replacement by more modern telescopes like JWST.
hubble.fun_fact: Hubble took the 'Hubble Deep Field' image, showing thousands of galaxies in a tiny patch of sky the size of a grain of sand.

ligo.name: LIGO
ligo.description: Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory—a detector capable of sensing ripples in spacetime from catastrophic events. Like a microphone listening to the Universe in gravitational waves.
ligo.history: Idea proposed in the 1970s. Construction began in 1994. First direct detection of gravitational waves occurred on September 14, 2015 (black hole merger). The 2017 Nobel Prize was awarded for this discovery.
ligo.how_it_works: Two perpendicular arms 4 km long. A laser beam is split, reflected by mirrors, and interferes. A passing gravitational wave changes the arm lengths by a tiny amount, detected as a change in interference.
key_problems: Noise: seismic, thermal, quantum.
Need to increase sensitivity to detect more events.
ligo.fun_fact: LIGO can measure a change in arm length 10,000 times smaller than the diameter of a proton.

telescope.name: telescope
telescope.description: An instrument for collecting and focusing electromagnetic radiation from distant objects. Like a spyglass, but much larger and more complex. There are optical, radio, X-ray, and other types.
telescope.history: The first telescope was invented by Hans Lippershey in 1608. Galileo improved it and turned it to the sky. Newton built the first reflecting telescope. Since then, sizes have grown from a few centimeters to 10 meters.
telescope.how_it_works: A telescope collects light from a large area (lens or mirror) and focuses it into an image. The larger the diameter, the more light and higher resolution. Modern telescopes use adaptive optics to compensate for the atmosphere.
key_problems: Atmospheric distortion for ground-based telescopes.
Cost and complexity of building giant telescopes.
telescope.fun_fact: The largest optical telescope is the Gran Telescopio Canarias with a diameter of 10.4 meters.

formulas.meaning: Angular resolution is inversely proportional to telescope diameter D

superposition.name: Superposition
superposition.description: A state of a quantum system in which it simultaneously exists in all possible states until the moment of measurement. Imagine a coin spinning in the air: while it hasn't landed, it is both heads and tails. In the quantum world, a particle can be in two places at once until we look.
superposition.history: The concept of superposition dates back to the work of Erwin Schrödinger (1926), who formulated the wave equation. Later, Richard Feynman developed the idea in his path integrals. Superposition has been experimentally confirmed in experiments with photons and electrons.
superposition.how_it_works: A quantum object is described by a wave function, which is the sum of all possible states. For example, an electron in an atom can be in several orbitals simultaneously. It's like being both at the office and at the beach at the same time until a colleague calls and asks where you are.
key_problems: The problem of decoherence: how and why does superposition break down upon interaction with the environment?
The boundary between the quantum and classical worlds: why don't macroscopic objects exhibit superposition?
superposition.fun_fact: In 1999, Anton Zeilinger's group demonstrated superposition for C60 fullerene molecules, consisting of 60 carbon atoms.

formulas.meaning: The state of the system is the sum of two states with weights c1 and c2

quantum_entanglement.name: Quantum Entanglement
quantum_entanglement.description: A quantum phenomenon where two or more particles become interconnected so that the state of one instantly determines the state of the other, regardless of the distance between them. Imagine two dice: if you roll one and get a 6, the other will also show 6, even if it's on Mars.
quantum_entanglement.history: Einstein, Podolsky, and Rosen (EPR) in 1935 pointed out 'spooky action at a distance' as a paradox of quantum mechanics. John Bell in 1964 proposed inequalities to test entanglement. Alain Aspect's experiments (1982) confirmed the violation of Bell's inequalities, proving the reality of entanglement.
quantum_entanglement.how_it_works: When creating a pair of entangled photons, their polarizations are correlated. Measuring the polarization of one photon instantly determines the polarization of the other. This is not information transfer but correlation: like having two envelopes with identical socks—opening one reveals the color of the other.
key_problems: How to reconcile entanglement with the principle of locality in relativity?
Can entanglement be used for faster-than-light information transfer (so far, no)?
quantum_entanglement.fun_fact: Quantum entanglement is used in quantum cryptography: if someone tries to intercept a message, the entanglement is destroyed and detected.

formulas.meaning: Entangled state of two qubits: if the first is 0, the second is also 0, and vice versa

uncertainty_principle.name: Uncertainty Principle
uncertainty_principle.description: A fundamental law of quantum mechanics stating that it is impossible to simultaneously precisely measure certain pairs of quantities, such as a particle's position and momentum. The more accurately you know where a particle is, the less you know how fast it is moving. It's like trying to photograph a rapidly spinning fan: to see the blades, you need a short exposure, but then the motion is blurred.
uncertainty_principle.history: Werner Heisenberg formulated the principle in 1927. He showed that the product of the uncertainties in position and momentum cannot be less than Planck's constant. This became a key distinction between quantum mechanics and classical physics.
uncertainty_principle.how_it_works: When measuring a particle's position, we use photons that impart momentum to it, changing its speed. The more precise the position measurement, the greater the disturbance. This is not a technical limitation but a property of nature: particles do not have simultaneously definite positions and momenta.
key_problems: How to interpret uncertainty: as an objective property or as our ignorance? (debate between Copenhagen and other interpretations)
uncertainty_principle.fun_fact: The uncertainty principle explains why atoms do not collapse: electrons cannot fall into the nucleus because then their position would be precisely known, leading to huge momentum uncertainty.

formulas.meaning: The product of uncertainties in position and momentum is at least half the reduced Planck constant

quantum_tunneling.name: Quantum Tunneling
quantum_tunneling.description: A quantum phenomenon where a particle passes through a potential barrier that it could not overcome according to classical laws. Imagine running towards a wall, but instead of hitting it, you pass through it with some probability. In the quantum world, this happens constantly.
quantum_tunneling.history: Quantum tunneling was predicted by Friedrich Hund (1927) and developed by George Gamow (1928) to explain alpha decay. Gamow showed that particles can 'leak' through a nuclear barrier. Later, tunneling became the basis for the tunnel diode and scanning tunneling microscope.
quantum_tunneling.how_it_works: In quantum mechanics, a particle is described by a wave function that does not abruptly end at the barrier but decays exponentially inside it. If the barrier is thin enough, the wave function 'leaks' out, and the particle can appear on the other side. The tunneling probability depends on the particle's mass and the barrier's height.
key_problems: How to precisely describe tunneling in time? Does 'tunneling time' exist and can it be less than the time light takes to cross the barrier?
quantum_tunneling.fun_fact: Quantum tunneling is used in the scanning tunneling microscope (STM), which allows us to see individual atoms: the tip is brought so close that electrons tunnel through the gap, creating an image.

formulas.meaning: The tunneling probability is exponentially small and depends on the particle's mass and the barrier's width

waveparticle_duality.name: Wave-Particle Duality
waveparticle_duality.description: The property of quantum objects to exhibit both wave-like and particle-like properties depending on the experiment. Light can behave as a wave (interference) and as a stream of particles (photoelectric effect). It's as if a person were simultaneously a liquid and a solid depending on the situation.
waveparticle_duality.history: Christiaan Huygens (wave theory of light) and Isaac Newton (corpuscular theory) debated the nature of light. In 1905, Einstein explained the photoelectric effect by introducing photons. Louis de Broglie (1924) proposed that particles also have wave properties. Davisson and Germer's experiments (1927) confirmed the wave nature of electrons.
waveparticle_duality.how_it_works: In the double-slit experiment, electrons fired one by one create an interference pattern, like waves. But if a detector is placed, they behave like particles, passing through one slit. The choice of measurement determines which property appears. This is not a compromise but complementarity: both wave and particle are two sides of the same coin.
key_problems: How to unify wave and particle nature in a single theory? Some interpretations (e.g., Bohmian mechanics) attempt this, but debates remain.
waveparticle_duality.fun_fact: In 2015, scientists demonstrated the wave nature of molecules with over 800 atoms, a record for massive objects.

formulas.meaning: The wavelength of a particle is inversely proportional to its momentum

quantum_field.name: Quantum Field
quantum_field.description: A fundamental concept in which each particle is an excitation of a corresponding field that permeates all space. For example, an electron is an excitation of the electron field, a photon of the electromagnetic field. Imagine an ocean: waves (particles) are disturbances of the water (field).
quantum_field.history: Quantum field theory (QFT) emerged in the 1930s from the work of Dirac, Pauli, Feynman, Schwinger, and Tomonaga. It unified quantum mechanics and special relativity. Feynman developed diagrams for calculating interactions. The Standard Model is a quantum field theory for all known particles.
quantum_field.how_it_works: In QFT, particles are quanta of fields. The field can be in a ground state (vacuum) or an excited state (particle). Interactions occur through the exchange of virtual particles. For example, two electrons repel by exchanging virtual photons. The vacuum is not empty: virtual particle pairs constantly appear and disappear.
key_problems: How to unify quantum field theory with general relativity? (quantum gravity)
The hierarchy problem: why is gravity so weak compared to other forces?
quantum_field.fun_fact: The vacuum in quantum field theory is not empty: it seethes with virtual particles, leading to the Casimir effect—the attraction of two closely spaced plates in a vacuum.

formulas.meaning: Describes the interaction of electrons (ψ) and photons (field F)

wave_function_collapse.name: Wave Function Collapse
wave_function_collapse.description: The process by which a quantum system transitions from a superposition of all possible states to a single definite state as a result of measurement. It's like flipping a coin: while in the air, it is both heads and tails, but once caught, it becomes either heads or tails.
wave_function_collapse.history: The concept was introduced by Werner Heisenberg and Niels Bohr within the Copenhagen interpretation (1927). John von Neumann mathematically described collapse as the reduction of the wave packet. Later, alternative interpretations emerged: the many-worlds interpretation (Everett, 1957) denies collapse, while objective collapse (GRW) posits spontaneous localization.
wave_function_collapse.how_it_works: Before measurement, the system is described by a wave function that evolves deterministically according to the Schrödinger equation. Upon measurement, the wave function abruptly 'collapses' into one of the eigenstates of the measuring device. The probability of each outcome is given by the squared modulus of the amplitude. The mechanism of collapse remains unclear.
key_problems: What causes collapse? The observer's consciousness? Interaction with the environment? Or is there no collapse, but a splitting of universes? (the measurement problem)
wave_function_collapse.fun_fact: In Wheeler's delayed-choice experiment (1978), collapse can be 'delayed' by making a photon decide to behave as a wave or particle after passing through the slits.

formulas.description: Probability of collapse into state n
formulas.meaning: The probability of obtaining result n is the square of the projection of the wave function onto that state

quantum_teleportation.name: Quantum Teleportation
quantum_teleportation.description: The transfer of a quantum state of a particle over a distance using quantum entanglement and classical communication. The particle itself does not move, but its state is 'copied' onto another particle, destroying the original. It's like scanning a document, printing a copy, and burning the original—information is transferred, but not matter.
quantum_teleportation.history: Theoretically proposed by Bennett et al. in 1993. The first experimental realization was by Anton Zeilinger's group in 1997 for photons. Later, states of atoms, ions, and even qubits in superconducting circuits were teleported. The distance record is over 1400 km (Micius satellite, 2017).
quantum_teleportation.how_it_works: Alice has a particle A in an unknown state. She and Bob share an entangled pair of particles B and C. Alice performs a measurement on A and B, which destroys A's state and entangles B and C. Alice sends the measurement result to Bob via a classical channel. Bob applies a corresponding transformation to C, and C takes on the original state of A. Without classical communication, teleportation is impossible.
key_problems: How to increase the accuracy and distance of teleportation for complex systems? How to integrate teleportation into quantum networks?
quantum_teleportation.fun_fact: Quantum teleportation does not violate relativity because classical information is transmitted at or below the speed of light, and the state itself is teleported instantly but cannot be read without the classical key.

formulas.meaning: The state of particle C becomes identical to the original state of A

quantum_computer.name: Quantum Computer
quantum_computer.description: A computing device that uses quantum phenomena (superposition and entanglement) to process information. Unlike classical bits (0 or 1), quantum bits (qubits) can be in superposition, allowing parallel computations. It's like being able to read all the books in a library simultaneously, rather than one at a time.
quantum_computer.history: The idea of quantum computing dates back to Richard Feynman (1982) and Yuri Manin. Peter Shor (1994) developed a factoring algorithm threatening RSA encryption. Lov Grover (1996) developed a search algorithm. The first experimental quantum computers appeared in the late 1990s (ion traps, superconductors). Today, companies like Google and IBM build processors with dozens of qubits.
quantum_computer.how_it_works: Qubits are implemented on different physical platforms: trapped ions, superconducting circuits, photons. Operations on qubits are quantum gates, analogous to logic gates. Measuring a qubit yields 0 or 1 with probability determined by its state. Quantum algorithms use interference to amplify correct answers and suppress wrong ones.
key_problems: Decoherence: how to maintain a quantum state long enough for computations?
Scalability: how to create thousands and millions of qubits with low error rates?
quantum_computer.fun_fact: In 2019, Google claimed 'quantum supremacy': their Sycamore processor solved a task in 200 seconds that would take a classical supercomputer 10,000 years.

formulas.meaning: The qubit is in a superposition of states 0 and 1 with amplitudes α and β

dark_matter.name: Dark Matter
dark_matter.description: A hypothetical form of matter that does not interact with electromagnetic radiation but reveals itself through gravitational influence on visible matter. It constitutes about 27% of the mass-energy of the universe. Imagine an invisible mass that holds galaxies together like invisible glue.
dark_matter.history: First evidence was obtained by Fritz Zwicky (1933) while studying the Coma Cluster: he found that visible mass was insufficient to hold the galaxies together. Vera Rubin (1970s) showed that galaxy rotation curves are flat, indicating additional mass. Modern searches include experiments with WIMPs and axions.
dark_matter.how_it_works: Dark matter does not emit or absorb light, so it cannot be seen. It participates only in gravitational interaction. Its presence is detected by the motion of stars and galaxies, gravitational lensing, and the structure of the cosmic microwave background. It is thought to consist of slow massive particles (WIMPs) or light axions.
key_problems: What is dark matter made of? (no candidate particle has been detected yet)
How does it interact with ordinary matter besides gravity? (weak interaction?)
dark_matter.fun_fact: Dark matter is not just 'dark', it is completely transparent: if you could see it, you would see stars on the other side of a galaxy as if through a ghost.

dark_energy.name: Dark Energy
dark_energy.description: A mysterious form of energy permeating all space and causing the accelerated expansion of the universe. It constitutes about 68% of the total energy of the universe. Imagine the universe not just expanding, but accelerating, as if something is pushing it from within.
dark_energy.history: The discovery of the accelerated expansion of the universe was made in 1998 by two groups: the Supernova Cosmology Project (Perlmutter) and the High-Z Supernova Search Team (Riess, Schmidt). They observed type Ia supernovae and found they were dimmer than expected, indicating acceleration. This discovery earned the Nobel Prize in 2011.
dark_energy.how_it_works: Dark energy manifests as negative pressure, which counteracts gravity. Its nature is unknown; the simplest model is the cosmological constant Λ (vacuum energy). Other models include quintessence — a dynamic field. Dark energy has dominated the dynamics of the universe for the last 5 billion years.
key_problems: What is dark energy? (cosmological constant or dynamic field?)
Why is its density so small but not zero? (cosmological constant problem)
dark_energy.fun_fact: If dark energy were stronger in the early universe, galaxies could not have formed; if weaker, the universe might have collapsed.

formulas.description: Equation of state of dark energy
formulas.meaning: Ratio of pressure to density; for cosmological constant w = -1

expansion_of_the_universe.name: Expansion of the Universe
expansion_of_the_universe.description: A fundamental property of the universe, consisting of the increase in distances between galaxies over time. It is not the motion of galaxies in space, but the expansion of space itself. Imagine raisins in rising dough: the raisins (galaxies) move apart from each other, although they themselves do not move.
expansion_of_the_universe.history: Discovered by Edwin Hubble in 1929 based on observations of galactic redshifts. Theoretically predicted by Alexander Friedmann (1922) and Georges Lemaître (1927). In 1998, it was discovered that the expansion is accelerating (dark energy). Modern measurements of the Hubble constant give different values (Hubble tension).
expansion_of_the_universe.how_it_works: Hubble's law: the recession velocity of a galaxy is proportional to its distance. Redshift arises from the stretching of light waves as space expands. The expansion has no center: all points move away from each other. On small scales (within galaxies), gravity overcomes expansion, so they do not expand.
key_problems: Why does the expansion rate (Hubble constant) differ when measured by different methods? (Hubble tension)
Will expansion continue forever or turn into contraction? (depends on dark energy)
expansion_of_the_universe.fun_fact: The expansion of the universe does not mean Earth is moving away from the Sun: gravity holds the solar system together, and expansion manifests only on intergalactic scales.

formulas.meaning: Recession velocity of a galaxy is proportional to distance

cosmic_microwave_background.name: Cosmic Microwave Background
cosmic_microwave_background.description: Electromagnetic radiation filling the entire universe, left over from the early epoch of the Big Bang. It is thermal radiation with a temperature of about 2.725 K, corresponding to the microwave range. Imagine a faint glow that we see as 'noise' on an old TV — that is the echo of the birth of the universe.
cosmic_microwave_background.history: Discovered accidentally in 1965 by Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson (Nobel Prize 1978) as isotropic noise. Theoretically predicted by George Gamow, Ralph Alpher, and Robert Herman (1948). The COBE satellite (1989) showed the spectrum is blackbody, and WMAP (2001) and Planck (2009) measured anisotropy with high precision.
cosmic_microwave_background.how_it_works: About 380,000 years after the Big Bang, the universe cooled enough for protons and electrons to form hydrogen atoms (recombination). Photons stopped scattering and began to travel freely. Since then, they have cooled due to the expansion of the universe to 2.7 K. Anisotropy (temperature fluctuations) reflects inhomogeneities in the early universe, from which galaxies formed.
key_problems: Why is the cosmic microwave background so uniform? (horizon problem, solved by inflation)
What can polarization modes (B-modes) tell us about gravitational waves from inflation?
cosmic_microwave_background.fun_fact: The cosmic microwave background is the oldest radiation that can be observed: its photons have been traveling to us for almost 13.8 billion years.

formulas.meaning: Spectral density of blackbody radiation at temperature T

multiverse.name: Multiverse
multiverse.description: A hypothetical set of multiple universes, including ours, which may have different physical laws and constants. The idea arises from various theories: quantum mechanics (many-worlds interpretation), cosmology (eternal inflation), and string theory (landscape). It is like an infinite library where each book is a separate universe.
multiverse.history: The many-worlds interpretation of quantum mechanics was proposed by Hugh Everett (1957). The idea of eternal inflation was developed by Andrei Linde (1980s). String theory predicts a huge number of possible vacua (landscape). The anthropic principle is used to explain the fine-tuning of physical constants. There is no direct evidence for the multiverse.
multiverse.how_it_works: In eternal inflation, different regions of spacetime stop expanding at different times, forming 'pocket universes' with different properties. In the many-worlds interpretation, each quantum measurement splits the universe into branches. String theory allows up to 10^500 different vacua. Testing the hypothesis is difficult because other universes may be unobservable.
key_problems: How can the existence of the multiverse be tested? (are there observable signals, e.g., bubble collisions?)
Is the multiverse a scientific hypothesis or a philosophical speculation? (falsifiability problem)
multiverse.fun_fact: If the multiverse exists, in some universes you might be president, and in others you might never be born; but we can never verify it.

entropy.name: Entropy
entropy.description: A measure of disorder or uncertainty in a system. Imagine a room: when things are neatly arranged, entropy is low; when they are scattered, it is high. The second law of thermodynamics states that in an isolated system, entropy always increases.
entropy.history: The concept was introduced by Rudolf Clausius in 1865. Ludwig Boltzmann gave a statistical definition: entropy is proportional to the logarithm of the number of microstates. In 1948, Claude Shannon applied entropy to information theory.
entropy.how_it_works: Entropy increases because the probability of a chaotic state is much higher than that of an ordered one. Analogy: a deck of cards — if shuffled, order disappears. In cosmology, the entropy of the universe constantly increases, which is linked to the arrow of time.
key_problems: How to explain the low entropy of the early universe? Is entropy related to information and consciousness?
entropy.fun_fact: The entropy of a black hole is proportional to the area of its event horizon, not its volume, hinting at the holographic principle.

formulas.meaning: Entropy S equals Boltzmann's constant k times the natural logarithm of the number of microstates Ω.

antimatter.name: Antimatter
antimatter.description: Matter composed of antiparticles, which have the same mass but opposite charge and other quantum numbers. When a particle meets its antiparticle, they annihilate, releasing energy. Imagine that every person has an exact copy with opposite qualities — upon meeting, they vanish in a flash of light.
antimatter.history: Paul Dirac predicted the existence of the antielectron (positron) in 1928 by combining quantum mechanics and special relativity. The positron was discovered by Carl Anderson in 1932. Later, the antiproton and antineutron were discovered.
antimatter.how_it_works: In laboratories, antiparticles are created in accelerators during high-energy particle collisions. Analogy: if you strike an anvil with a hammer, sparks may fly — similarly, collisions produce particle-antiparticle pairs. In nature, antimatter is rare because it quickly annihilates with ordinary matter.
key_problems: Why is there so little antimatter in the universe? This is called baryon asymmetry — one of the greatest mysteries in physics.
antimatter.fun_fact: In the movie 'Angels & Demons', antimatter is used as a bomb — in reality, producing even a gram of antimatter would cost billions of dollars and take years.

formulas.meaning: When mass m annihilates, energy E is released, equal to mass times the speed of light squared.

string_theory.name: String Theory
string_theory.description: A hypothetical theory in which fundamental particles are not points but one-dimensional vibrating strings. Different vibration frequencies of the string give rise to different particles. Imagine that all particles are notes played by a violinist on a single string: the melody depends on how the string vibrates.
string_theory.history: String theory originated in the late 1960s to describe the strong interaction. In the 1980s, after the first superstring revolution, it became clear that it could unify all fundamental interactions. The second revolution in the 1990s led to M-theory.
string_theory.how_it_works: Strings have a size on the order of the Planck length (10^-35 m). They can be open (free ends) or closed (loops). String vibrations determine the mass and charge of a particle. Analogy: a guitar string — different ways of plucking produce different sounds.
key_problems: String theory has no experimental confirmation, as it requires energies inaccessible to current accelerators. Moreover, it predicts many extra dimensions that are not observed.
string_theory.fun_fact: String theory predicts the existence of the graviton — a particle that mediates gravity, making it a candidate for a theory of everything.

standard_model.name: Standard Model
standard_model.description: A theory describing three of the four fundamental interactions (electromagnetic, weak, and strong) and all known elementary particles. It is like the periodic table but for the subatomic world: it systematizes quarks, leptons, bosons, and their interactions.
standard_model.history: The Standard Model was formed in the 1960s-1970s thanks to the work of Glashow, Weinberg, and Salam (electroweak unification). The discovery of the Higgs boson in 2012 completed its particle content. However, it does not include gravity or dark matter.
standard_model.how_it_works: Particles are divided into fermions (matter) and bosons (force carriers). Quarks form protons and neutrons, leptons form electrons and neutrinos. Analogy: it is like the rules of chess — the pieces (particles) and their moves (interactions) are precisely described.
key_problems: The Standard Model does not explain dark matter, dark energy, or gravity. It also does not answer the question of neutrino masses.
standard_model.fun_fact: The Standard Model predicted the existence of many particles, including W and Z bosons, gluons, and the top quark, long before their experimental discovery.

higgs_boson.name: Higgs Boson
higgs_boson.description: A fundamental particle responsible for the mechanism by which other particles acquire mass. It is the quantum of the Higgs field, which permeates all space. Imagine the Higgs field as a thick molasses, and particles as balls: some move easily, others get stuck, giving them mass.
higgs_boson.history: Peter Higgs and other physicists independently proposed the mechanism in 1964. The Higgs boson was discovered in 2012 at the Large Hadron Collider (CERN), confirming the Standard Model. Peter Higgs and François Englert received the Nobel Prize in 2013.
higgs_boson.how_it_works: Particles interact with the Higgs field, and the stronger the interaction, the greater the mass. Analogy: a celebrity in a crowd — their surroundings (field) slow them down, creating an effect of mass. Without the Higgs field, all particles would be massless and move at the speed of light.
key_problems: Why is the Higgs boson mass what it is? This is related to the hierarchy problem. It is also unclear whether the Higgs boson is elementary or composite.
higgs_boson.fun_fact: The Higgs boson is sometimes called the 'God particle' — a name physicists dislike, but it has stuck in popular culture.

quarkgluon_plasma.name: Quark-Gluon Plasma
quarkgluon_plasma.description: A state of matter in which quarks and gluons, normally confined inside protons and neutrons, become free. This is the hottest and densest matter, existing microseconds after the Big Bang. Imagine ice melting into water — similarly, hadrons 'melt' into plasma at extreme temperatures.
quarkgluon_plasma.history: The existence of quark-gluon plasma was predicted in the 1970s. In the 2000s, experimental evidence was obtained at the RHIC collider at Brookhaven and the Large Hadron Collider at CERN. The substance behaves as a nearly perfect fluid with very low viscosity.
quarkgluon_plasma.how_it_works: At temperatures above 2 trillion degrees Celsius (100,000 times hotter than the center of the Sun), protons and neutrons 'melt', releasing quarks and gluons. Analogy: heating ice first melts it, then water evaporates — similarly, nuclear matter transitions to plasma.
key_problems: How to accurately describe the properties of quark-gluon plasma? It behaves as a liquid, not a gas, contrary to expectations.
quarkgluon_plasma.fun_fact: Quark-gluon plasma has the lowest viscosity among known liquids, close to the theoretical limit.

gravity.name: Gravity
gravity.description: A fundamental interaction that attracts all bodies with mass and energy. It governs the motion of planets, the fall of an apple, and the structure of the universe. Imagine spacetime as a stretched rubber sheet, and massive objects dent it, causing other bodies to roll into the depressions.
gravity.history: Isaac Newton formulated the law of universal gravitation in 1687. Albert Einstein replaced it with general relativity in 1915, where gravity is the curvature of spacetime. In 2015, gravitational waves were discovered, confirming Einstein's prediction.
gravity.how_it_works: Gravity acts at any distance, but its strength decreases with the square of the distance. Analogy: the farther you are from a light source, the dimmer it is — similarly, gravity weakens. In GR, massive objects curve spacetime, and bodies move along geodesics.
key_problems: Gravity does not fit into quantum mechanics. Creating a quantum theory of gravity is one of the main challenges of modern physics.
gravity.fun_fact: Gravity is the weakest of the four fundamental interactions: a refrigerator magnet easily overcomes the gravity of the entire Earth.

formulas.meaning: The gravitational force between two bodies is proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

electromagnetism.name: Electromagnetism
electromagnetism.description: A fundamental interaction between electrically charged particles. It is responsible for light, radio waves, chemical bonds, and electronics. Imagine electricity and magnetism as two sides of the same coin: moving charges create a magnetic field, and a changing magnetic field generates an electric field.
electromagnetism.history: James Clerk Maxwell unified electricity and magnetism into a single theory in the 1860s, predicting electromagnetic waves. Heinrich Hertz experimentally confirmed their existence in 1887. Later, quantum electrodynamics (QED) described the interaction at the quantum level.
electromagnetism.how_it_works: The electromagnetic interaction is mediated by photons. It acts on charged particles such as electrons and protons. Analogy: two magnets can attract or repel — similarly, electric charges behave. Electromagnetism is billions of times stronger than gravity.
key_problems: How to unify electromagnetism with other interactions into a single theory? This is the goal of grand unified theories.
electromagnetism.fun_fact: Light is an electromagnetic wave, and different colors correspond to different frequencies of oscillation of electric and magnetic fields.

formulas.meaning: The force between two point charges is proportional to the product of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance.

nuclear_fusion.name: Nuclear Fusion
nuclear_fusion.description: A process in which light atomic nuclei merge to form a heavier nucleus, releasing enormous amounts of energy. It is the energy source of the Sun and stars. Imagine two ping-pong balls sticking together into one larger ball, releasing energy — similarly, nuclei overcome repulsion and fuse.
nuclear_fusion.history: The idea of nuclear fusion as the source of stellar energy was proposed by Arthur Eddington in the 1920s. Hans Bethe described the proton-proton chain in 1939. The hydrogen bomb (1952) demonstrated uncontrolled fusion. Currently, work is underway on controlled thermonuclear fusion (ITER).
nuclear_fusion.how_it_works: Fusion requires extreme temperatures (millions of degrees) and pressure to overcome the Coulomb repulsion of nuclei. Analogy: to bring two strong magnets together with like poles, you need to apply force. In stars, fusion occurs in the core, converting hydrogen into helium.
key_problems: How to create a stable controlled thermonuclear fusion on Earth? The problem of plasma confinement and achieving a positive energy output.
nuclear_fusion.fun_fact: One gram of hydrogen fuel in a fusion reaction yields as much energy as burning 11 tons of coal.

formulas.meaning: Deuterium and tritium nuclei fuse to form helium-4, a neutron, and release 17.6 MeV of energy.

radioactivity.name: Radioactivity
radioactivity.description: Spontaneous transformation of unstable atomic nuclei into other nuclei, accompanied by emission of particles or electromagnetic radiation. This phenomenon underlies nuclear energy, medicine (radiation therapy), and dating. Imagine a nucleus as an unstable tower of blocks that eventually collapses, throwing out blocks.
radioactivity.history: Antoine Henri Becquerel discovered radioactivity in 1896 while studying uranium salts. Marie and Pierre Curie isolated new radioactive elements—polonium and radium. Ernest Rutherford identified alpha and beta radiation, and in 1911 proposed the planetary model of the atom.
radioactivity.how_it_works: Radioactive decay occurs randomly, but for a large number of atoms, the half-life is constant. Analogy: popcorn in a microwave—you don't know which kernel will pop next, but you know that after a certain time half will pop. There are alpha, beta, and gamma decay.
key_problems: The problem of safe disposal of radioactive waste for thousands of years. Also, the mechanisms of some rare types of decay are not fully understood.
radioactivity.fun_fact: Radioactivity is used to determine the age of archaeological finds (radiocarbon dating)—up to 50,000 years.

formulas.meaning: The number of undecayed nuclei N decreases exponentially with time t, where λ is the decay constant.

photon.name: Photon
photon.description: An elementary particle, the quantum of electromagnetic radiation. Photons have no mass, always move at the speed of light, and carry energy proportional to frequency. Imagine light as a stream of tiny bullets, each carrying a certain amount of energy—these are photons.
photon.history: The idea of light quanta was introduced by Max Planck in 1900 to explain blackbody radiation. Albert Einstein in 1905 explained the photoelectric effect, assuming light consists of photons. For this he won the Nobel Prize. Later, quantum electrodynamics described the interaction of photons with matter.
photon.how_it_works: Photons are emitted and absorbed by atoms during electron transitions between energy levels. Analogy: if you throw a stone into water, waves form—so an electron transitioning to a lower level emits a photon. Photons exhibit wave-particle duality.
key_problems: How to control single photons for quantum computing and communication? The problem of decoherence and photon loss.
photon.fun_fact: A photon has no mass but has momentum: sunlight exerts a pressure on Earth's surface of about 10 micronewtons per square meter.

formulas.meaning: The energy of a photon equals Planck's constant h times the frequency ν.

electron.name: Electron
electron.description: A stable elementary particle with a negative electric charge. Electrons form the electron shells of atoms and determine the chemical properties of elements. They participate in electric current, thermal conductivity, and many other phenomena. Imagine electrons as tiny balls orbiting the atomic nucleus like planets around the Sun.
electron.history: The electron was discovered by Joseph John Thomson in 1897 while studying cathode rays. He determined the charge-to-mass ratio. Robert Millikan measured the electron's charge in 1909. Later, quantum mechanics described the electron as a wave rather than a particle.
electron.how_it_works: Electrons occupy orbitals—regions of space around the nucleus where the probability of finding them is highest. Analogy: a bee buzzing around a flower—its position is fuzzy, but there is an area where it is most often. Electrons can transition between orbitals by absorbing or emitting photons.
key_problems: What is electron spin? It is an intrinsic property with no classical analogy. Also, it is unclear whether the electron is a point particle or has structure.
electron.fun_fact: Electrons have no known internal structure and are considered truly elementary particles—they are not made of smaller particles.

proton.name: Proton
proton.description: A stable subatomic particle with a positive electric charge, found in atomic nuclei. The number of protons determines the chemical element. Protons consist of two up quarks and one down quark. Imagine a proton as a tough nut, inside which three quarks are hidden, held together by the strong interaction.
proton.history: Ernest Rutherford discovered the proton in 1919 by bombarding nitrogen with alpha particles. He suggested that the hydrogen nucleus (proton) is part of other nuclei. Later, James Chadwick discovered the neutron, and the model of the nucleus became clear. In the 1960s, it was established that the proton consists of quarks.
proton.how_it_works: Protons are held in the nucleus by the strong nuclear force, which overcomes electrical repulsion. Analogy: several magnets attracted to each other despite repelling—so protons are bound by the strong force. The proton's size is about 0.84 femtometers.
key_problems: The exact size of the proton is a subject of debate: different methods give slightly different values (proton radius puzzle). Also, the detailed structure of the proton is unclear.
proton.fun_fact: Protons are stable, but some theories predict their decay with a half-life of more than 10^34 years—this has not yet been observed.

neutron.name: Neutron
neutron.description: A neutral subatomic particle found in atomic nuclei together with protons. Neutrons are stable only inside the nucleus; a free neutron decays in about 15 minutes. They consist of one up quark and two down quarks. Imagine a neutron as the 'glue' that helps protons coexist in the nucleus without flying apart.
neutron.history: James Chadwick discovered the neutron in 1932 by bombarding beryllium with alpha particles. This discovery allowed understanding of the nucleus and led to the creation of the nuclear reactor. Enrico Fermi used neutrons to induce radioactivity.
neutron.how_it_works: Neutrons participate in the strong nuclear interaction, holding the nucleus together. Analogy: in a group of friends (protons), there is a peacemaker (neutron) who smooths over conflicts. Neutrons are also used to initiate the chain reaction of uranium fission.
key_problems: Why is a free neutron unstable while a bound one in a nucleus is stable? This is related to nuclear energetics.
neutron.fun_fact: Neutron stars are remnants of supernova explosions, consisting mostly of neutrons; their density is such that a teaspoon of matter weighs billions of tons.

quark.name: Quark
quark.description: A fundamental particle that makes up protons, neutrons, and other hadrons. Quarks have fractional electric charge and participate in the strong interaction. There are six types (flavors): up, down, strange, charm, bottom, and top. Imagine quarks as bricks from which all matter particles are built.
quark.history: The quark hypothesis was independently proposed by Murray Gell-Mann and George Zweig in 1964. Gell-Mann introduced the term 'quark' from the novel 'Finnegans Wake'. Experimental confirmation of quarks was obtained in 1968 at the Stanford Linear Accelerator.
quark.how_it_works: Quarks never appear alone due to confinement—they are always bound in hadrons. Analogy: try to separate one magnet from a chain—you cannot get a single magnet, only pairs. Quarks are held together by gluons, particles of the strong interaction.
key_problems: Why do quarks not exist freely? The mechanism of confinement is not fully understood. Also, it is unclear why there are exactly six quark flavors.
quark.fun_fact: The heaviest quark—top—weighs as much as a gold atom but lives only 10^-25 seconds.

lepton.name: Lepton
lepton.description: A class of fundamental particles that do not participate in the strong interaction. Leptons include the electron, muon, tau lepton, and corresponding neutrinos. They are fermions with spin 1/2. Imagine leptons as solo dancers who do not form groups, unlike quarks.
lepton.history: The electron was discovered first (1897). The muon was found in 1936, the tau lepton in 1975. Neutrinos were predicted by Wolfgang Pauli in 1930 and discovered in 1956. The Standard Model includes three generations of leptons.
lepton.how_it_works: Leptons participate in electromagnetic and weak interactions. Analogy: leptons are different types of balls: electron—small, muon—medium, tau—large, and neutrinos—almost invisible. Neutrinos interact very weakly with matter, passing through Earth almost unimpeded.
key_problems: Why are there three generations of leptons? This is the fermion hierarchy puzzle. Also, the nature of neutrino mass is unclear.
lepton.fun_fact: Every second, about 100 billion neutrinos from the Sun pass through your body, but you don't notice them.

boson.name: Boson
boson.description: A particle with integer spin, obeying Bose-Einstein statistics. Bosons are carriers of fundamental interactions: photon (electromagnetism), gluons (strong), W and Z (weak), graviton (hypothetical). The Higgs boson is a scalar boson. Imagine bosons as mail carriers delivering messages between particles.
