boson.history: Statistics for bosons were developed by Satyendra Nath Bose and Albert Einstein in 1924. The photon as a boson was known earlier. W and Z bosons were discovered in 1983, the Higgs boson in 2012. Gluons were postulated in the 1970s.
boson.how_it_works: Bosons can occupy the same quantum state, leading to phenomena such as lasers and Bose-Einstein condensates. Analogy: in a hall, bosons are people who can sit on the same chair without conflict. Fermions are like people each requiring a separate seat.
key_problems: Does the graviton exist? Its detection would require a detector the size of a planet. Also, it is unclear why the Higgs boson has such a small mass.
boson.fun_fact: Bosons can form a Bose-Einstein condensate—a state of matter where all particles behave as a single giant wave.

fermion.name: Fermion
fermion.description: A particle with half-integer spin, obeying the Pauli exclusion principle: two fermions cannot occupy the same quantum state. Fermions include quarks and leptons, as well as composite particles (protons, neutrons). Imagine fermions as people on a bus: each takes a separate seat, and two people cannot sit in one.
fermion.history: The exclusion principle was formulated by Wolfgang Pauli in 1925. Fermi-Dirac statistics were developed by Enrico Fermi and Paul Dirac in 1926. Fermions constitute matter, while bosons are force carriers.
fermion.how_it_works: Due to the Pauli principle, fermions form the structure of atoms: electrons fill shells, preventing collapse. Analogy: parking—only one car can occupy a spot. This property explains the stability of matter and the diversity of chemical elements.
key_problems: Why is there an asymmetry between matter and antimatter, if fermions and antifermions should be created in pairs?
fermion.fun_fact: White dwarfs and neutron stars are prevented from collapsing by the pressure of degenerate fermions—electrons or neutrons.

hydrogen.name: hydrogen
hydrogen.description: Hydrogen is the lightest and most abundant element in the universe. Its atom consists of one proton and one electron. Imagine the simplest construction set: one ball (proton) and one small ball (electron) orbiting around it — that's hydrogen.
hydrogen.history: Hydrogen was discovered by English scientist Henry Cavendish in 1766 as 'flammable air.' Antoine Lavoisier named it hydrogène (water-builder) because burning hydrogen produces water. In 1931, Harold Urey discovered deuterium — an isotope of hydrogen.
hydrogen.how_it_works: Hydrogen is the primary fuel of stars. In stellar cores at millions of degrees, hydrogen fuses into helium through nuclear fusion, releasing enormous energy. On Earth, hydrogen is used in the chemical industry and is considered an eco-friendly fuel.
key_problems: Storing hydrogen in a compact form for transportation remains a technological challenge due to its low density.
hydrogen.fun_fact: Hydrogen is the only element that can exist in three isotopes with their own names: protium (ordinary hydrogen), deuterium, and tritium.

formulas.meaning: The emission frequency of neutral hydrogen, used in radio astronomy for mapping galaxies.

helium.name: helium
helium.description: Helium is the second lightest and second most abundant element in the universe. It is an inert gas with no color or odor. Imagine inflating a balloon: if filled with helium, it rises because helium is lighter than air.
helium.history: Helium was first detected in 1868 by astronomer Pierre Janssen during a solar eclipse as an unknown spectral line in the Sun's spectrum. The name comes from the Greek 'Helios' — Sun. On Earth, helium was isolated from uranium ore in 1895 by William Ramsay.
helium.how_it_works: Helium is produced in stars through nuclear fusion of hydrogen. It is also a product of radioactive decay of uranium and thorium in the Earth's crust. Helium is used in cryogenics (cooling superconductors), in breathing mixtures for deep-sea diving, and as a shielding gas in welding.
key_problems: Earth's helium reserves are limited and non-renewable; wasteful use may lead to future shortages.
helium.fun_fact: Helium is the only element that remains liquid at atmospheric pressure down to absolute zero, not solidifying without additional pressure.

carbon.name: carbon
carbon.description: Carbon is a chemical element, the basis of life on Earth. Its atoms can bond with each other and with other elements, forming millions of different compounds. Imagine a LEGO set: carbon is the universal piece from which you can build anything, from simple molecules to complex proteins.
carbon.history: Carbon has been known since ancient times in the form of charcoal and diamonds. In 1779, Antoine Lavoisier showed that diamond and graphite consist of the same element. In the 20th century, new forms of carbon were discovered: fullerenes, graphene, and carbon nanotubes.
carbon.how_it_works: Carbon forms four chemical bonds, allowing it to create long chains and rings. In stars, carbon is synthesized from helium in the triple-alpha process. On Earth, carbon is a component of all organic molecules: carbohydrates, fats, proteins, and DNA.
key_problems: Understanding the mechanisms of abiogenic synthesis of carbon-containing molecules in space and on early Earth.
carbon.fun_fact: Diamond and graphite are both forms of carbon, but diamond is the hardest natural substance, while graphite is soft and used in pencils.

oxygen.name: Oxygen
oxygen.description: Oxygen is a chemical element necessary for the respiration of most living organisms on Earth. Its molecule O₂ makes up about 21% of the atmosphere. Imagine taking a deep breath: the air you inhale contains oxygen, which your body uses to obtain energy from food.
oxygen.history: Oxygen was independently discovered by Joseph Priestley in 1774 and Carl Scheele (around 1771). Antoine Lavoisier named it 'oxygen' (from Greek 'acid-former'), although it was later found that not all acids contain oxygen. Lavoisier also explained the role of oxygen in combustion and respiration.
oxygen.how_it_works: Oxygen is a highly reactive element. It supports combustion and oxidation processes. In stars, oxygen is formed through nuclear fusion of carbon and helium. On Earth, oxygen is released by plants during photosynthesis and used by animals for respiration.
key_problems: The accumulation of oxygen in Earth's atmosphere (Great Oxidation Event) about 2.4 billion years ago is not fully explained.
oxygen.fun_fact: Oxygen is the third most abundant element in the universe after hydrogen and helium, but on Earth it is the most abundant by mass (about 50% of the Earth's crust).

iron.name: Iron
iron.description: Iron is a heavy metal that plays a key role in stellar evolution and life on Earth. Its nucleus is one of the most stable in nature. Imagine holding a nail in your hand: it is strong, heavy, and can rust — that is iron.
iron.history: Iron has been known to humanity since ancient times; the Iron Age began around 1200 BCE. In the 19th century, it was established that iron is the most abundant element in the composition of the Earth (by mass). In astrophysics, iron is the final product of thermonuclear fusion in massive stars.
iron.how_it_works: In stars, iron is formed through the fusion of silicon and other elements. Further fusion of iron into heavier elements requires energy rather than releasing it, so a star with an iron core collapses, triggering a supernova explosion. On Earth, iron is a component of hemoglobin, which carries oxygen in the blood.
key_problems: The exact mechanism of iron formation in supernovae and its distribution in galaxies.
iron.fun_fact: The Earth's iron core is in a liquid state and generates a magnetic field that protects us from cosmic radiation.

water.name: Water
water.description: Water (H₂O) is a simple compound of hydrogen and oxygen, but the basis of life on Earth. It exists in three states: liquid, solid (ice), and gaseous (vapor). Imagine drinking a glass of water: it is colorless, odorless, but life is impossible without it.
water.history: Water was recognized as a chemical compound in the late 18th century. Antoine Lavoisier and Henry Cavendish showed that water consists of hydrogen and oxygen. In the 20th century, water became an object of search on other planets, as its presence is considered a key condition for life.
water.how_it_works: Water is a universal solvent due to the polarity of its molecules. It participates in chemical reactions and regulates temperature due to its high heat capacity. In space, water has been found as ice on comets, moons of giant planets, and in molecular clouds.
key_problems: The origin of water on Earth: was it brought by comets or released from the planet's interior?
water.fun_fact: Water is one of the few substances whose solid phase (ice) is less dense than the liquid phase, so ice floats, preventing water bodies from freezing solid.

formulas.meaning: A water molecule consists of two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom.

methane.name: Methane
methane.description: Methane (CH₄) is the simplest hydrocarbon, a colorless and odorless gas. It is the main component of natural gas. Imagine lighting a gas stove: the blue flame is the combustion of methane.
methane.history: Methane was discovered by Italian scientist Alessandro Volta in 1776 while studying marsh gases. Its composition was established in the 19th century. In the 20th century, methane became an important fuel and object of study in planetology — it has been detected in the atmospheres of giant planets and on Titan.
methane.how_it_works: Methane is produced by the decomposition of organic matter without access to oxygen (anaerobic decomposition). In Earth's atmosphere, it is a greenhouse gas. On Titan, Saturn's moon, methane forms lakes and rivers and participates in a methane cycle analogous to the water cycle on Earth.
key_problems: Sources and sinks of methane on Earth are not fully accounted for, affecting climate models.
methane.fun_fact: On Titan, methane plays the same role as water on Earth: there are methane rains, rivers, and lakes, but at a temperature of -180°C.

formulas.meaning: A methane molecule consists of one carbon atom and four hydrogen atoms.

ammonia.name: Ammonia
ammonia.description: Ammonia (NH₃) is a compound of nitrogen and hydrogen, a gas with a pungent odor. It is widely used in fertilizers and household chemicals. Imagine the smell of ammonia solution: that is ammonia dissolved in water.
ammonia.history: Ammonia was known to alchemists, but first isolated in pure form by Joseph Priestley in 1774. In 1909, Fritz Haber developed a process for synthesizing ammonia from nitrogen and hydrogen (Haber-Bosch process), enabling industrial-scale fertilizer production.
ammonia.how_it_works: Ammonia is formed in nature from the decomposition of organic matter. In space, ammonia has been detected in the atmospheres of giant planets (Jupiter, Saturn) and in interstellar clouds. It plays a role in the chemistry of prebiotic molecules and may be an indicator of life.
key_problems: The role of ammonia in the origin of life: could it have been a source of nitrogen for the first organic molecules?
ammonia.fun_fact: Ammonia has a high heat of vaporization, so it is used in some refrigeration systems, although it is toxic.

formulas.meaning: An ammonia molecule consists of one nitrogen atom and three hydrogen atoms.

carbon_dioxide.name: Carbon dioxide
carbon_dioxide.description: Carbon dioxide (CO₂) is a gas composed of carbon and oxygen. It is a product of respiration and combustion, and an important greenhouse gas. Imagine exhaling: the exhaled air contains carbon dioxide, which plants use for photosynthesis.
carbon_dioxide.history: Carbon dioxide was discovered by Joseph Black in the 1750s, who called it 'fixed air'. Antoine Lavoisier showed that it consists of carbon and oxygen. In the 19th century, its role in the greenhouse effect was predicted by Svante Arrhenius.
carbon_dioxide.how_it_works: Carbon dioxide absorbs infrared radiation, trapping heat in the atmosphere — the greenhouse effect. In space, CO₂ has been detected in the atmospheres of Mars and Venus, creating a strong greenhouse effect there. On Earth, its concentration is rising due to the burning of fossil fuels.
key_problems: How to stabilize the level of CO₂ in the atmosphere to avoid catastrophic climate change?
carbon_dioxide.fun_fact: Venus has a dense atmosphere of CO₂, creating a surface temperature of about 460°C — hotter than Mercury, even though Venus is farther from the Sun.

formulas.meaning: A carbon dioxide molecule consists of one carbon atom and two oxygen atoms.

silicon.name: Silicon
silicon.description: Silicon is a semiconducting element, the basis of modern electronics. It is the second most abundant element in the Earth's crust after oxygen. Imagine your smartphone: its processor is made of silicon, which controls all computations.
silicon.history: Silicon was isolated in pure form by Jöns Jacob Berzelius in 1824. In the mid-20th century, the development of semiconductor technology made silicon a key material for transistors and microchips. In astrophysics, silicon is important as a product of stellar nucleosynthesis.
silicon.how_it_works: Silicon forms a crystal lattice that can be doped with impurities to create semiconducting properties. In stars, silicon is synthesized during the silicon-burning process, preceding the formation of iron. On Earth, silicon is a component of sand (SiO₂) and many minerals.
key_problems: Search for alternatives to silicon for future electronics, as silicon transistors are approaching physical limits of miniaturization.
silicon.fun_fact: Silicon is considered a possible basis for silicon-based life, although its chemistry is less diverse than that of carbon.

ice.name: Ice
ice.description: Ice is the solid phase of water. It forms when water freezes at 0°C and has the unique property that its density is lower than that of liquid water, so it floats. Imagine an ice cube in a glass: it does not sink but floats on the surface.
ice.history: Ice has been known to humanity since ancient times. In the 19th century, Michael Faraday studied the processes of melting and regelation (freezing under pressure). In the 20th century, various crystalline forms of ice (polymorphs) were discovered under high pressures. In astronomy, ice has been detected on many bodies in the Solar System.
ice.how_it_works: Ice forms when water loses heat and its molecules arrange into a hexagonal crystal lattice. In space, ice is a major component of comets, icy moons (Europa, Enceladus), and the outer parts of protoplanetary disks. Ice can contain gas impurities (clathrates).
key_problems: Is there life in the subsurface oceans of icy moons such as Europa or Enceladus?
ice.fun_fact: Ice can exist in 18 different crystalline forms, as well as in an amorphous state, which is found in the interstellar medium.

dust.name: Dust
dust.description: Dust in astronomy refers to small solid particles ranging in size from nanometers to micrometers, composed of silicates, carbon, and ice. It scatters and absorbs light, making cosmic objects appear dim. Imagine looking through a dusty window: the light becomes blurred and reddish.
dust.history: Cosmic dust was first detected by its effect on starlight. In the 19th century, Wilhelm Tempel noticed dark nebulae. In the 20th century, dust became an object of study in infrared astronomy. It is now known that dust plays a key role in the formation of stars and planets.
dust.how_it_works: Dust forms in the atmospheres of cool stars and in supernova ejecta. It condenses into solid particles, which then disperse into the interstellar medium. Dust absorbs ultraviolet and visible radiation, re-emitting it in the infrared, allowing the study of cold regions.
key_problems: The exact chemical composition and evolution of dust in various astrophysical environments.
dust.fun_fact: Cosmic dust is the building material for planets: from it, planetesimals form, and then planets.

amino_acids.name: Amino acids
amino_acids.description: Amino acids are organic molecules that are the building blocks of proteins. They consist of an amino group, a carboxyl group, and a side chain. Imagine proteins as long chains of colorful beads, where each bead is an amino acid.
amino_acids.history: The first amino acid (asparagine) was discovered in 1806. By the early 20th century, 20 standard amino acids were identified. In 1953, Stanley Miller conducted an experiment showing that amino acids could form under conditions of early Earth. In space, amino acids have been found in meteorites.
amino_acids.how_it_works: Amino acids join via peptide bonds to form proteins. In living organisms, they perform many functions: catalysis (enzymes), transport, structural support. In space, amino acids can be synthesized in interstellar clouds under the influence of ultraviolet radiation and cosmic rays.
key_problems: How did amino acids and other prebiotic molecules assemble into the first self-replicating systems?
amino_acids.fun_fact: In the Murchison meteorite, which fell in Australia in 1969, more than 70 different amino acids were found, including some not used by terrestrial life.

formulas.description: General formula of an amino acid
formulas.meaning: An amino acid contains an amino group (NH₂), a central carbon atom with a side chain R, and a carboxyl group (COOH).

phosphine.name: Phosphine
phosphine.description: Phosphine (PH₃) is a colorless, toxic gas with a smell of garlic or rotting fish. In nature, it forms under oxygen-free conditions, such as in swamps or during organic decomposition. In everyday life, it can be imagined as a 'chemical ghost' — invisible, but its presence is revealed by a sharp odor and the ability to glow in the dark upon contact with oxygen.
phosphine.history: Phosphine was first obtained in 1783 by French chemist Philippe Gengembre, but systematically studied later. In 2020, astronomers reported the detection of phosphine in Venus's atmosphere, sparking debates about possible biological sources, as on Earth phosphine is often associated with microbes. However, subsequent studies have cast doubt on this discovery.
phosphine.how_it_works: Phosphine forms during the decomposition of organic matter without access to oxygen — for example, in swamps or animal intestines. Industrially, it is produced from white phosphorus and alkali. Like matches that ignite by friction, phosphine is highly flammable in air, and when burned, it emits a characteristic green light.
key_problems: Is phosphine a reliable biomarker for searching for life on other planets? How to distinguish its biological origin from geochemical?
phosphine.fun_fact: Phosphine is so toxic that during World War I it was considered as a possible chemical weapon, but was rejected due to its high flammability.

formulas.description: Formation of phosphine from calcium phosphide
formulas.meaning: When calcium phosphide reacts with water, phosphine gas is released

ozone.name: Ozone
ozone.description: Ozone (O₃) is a bluish gas with a pungent odor, formed from ordinary oxygen under the influence of electrical discharges or ultraviolet light. Imagine oxygen as calm single molecules, and ozone as their aggressive version, capable of protecting us from harmful radiation but also irritating the lungs at high concentrations.
ozone.history: Ozone was discovered in 1840 by German chemist Christian Friedrich Schönbein, who noticed the characteristic odor during electrical experiments. In 1913, French physicists Charles Fabry and Henri Buisson proved the existence of the ozone layer in the stratosphere. In the 1970s, scientists discovered that chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) destroy ozone, leading to the Montreal Protocol in 1987.
ozone.how_it_works: Ozone forms when ultraviolet radiation from the Sun splits an oxygen molecule (O₂) into two atoms, each of which attaches to another O₂ molecule, forming O₃. This process occurs in the stratosphere at an altitude of 20–30 km, creating the ozone layer that absorbs harmful ultraviolet radiation. In everyday life, ozonators are used to disinfect water and air, but breathing ozone is harmful.
key_problems: How to effectively restore the ozone layer after its depletion? What are the long-term climate consequences of changes in stratospheric ozone concentration?
ozone.fun_fact: After a thunderstorm, you smell a fresh scent — this is ozone formed by lightning; its name comes from the Greek word 'ozein' (to smell).

formulas.description: Formation of ozone from oxygen under UV
formulas.meaning: Ultraviolet breaks an oxygen molecule, then atoms combine with other molecules to form ozone

nitrogen.name: Nitrogen
nitrogen.description: Nitrogen (N₂) is a colorless, odorless, and tasteless gas that makes up 78% of Earth's atmosphere. It is like an invisible 'background player': it does not support combustion or respiration, but is vital for plants in the form of compounds (e.g., ammonia). In everyday life, liquid nitrogen is used for freezing food, and the gas is used to create an inert atmosphere in welding.
nitrogen.history: Nitrogen was discovered in 1772 by Scottish physician Daniel Rutherford, who called it 'noxious air'. Almost simultaneously, it was studied by Antoine Lavoisier and other scientists. Lavoisier proposed the name 'azote' (from Greek 'lifeless'), because animals died in pure nitrogen. Later, it was found that nitrogen is a component of proteins and DNA, making it a key element of life.
nitrogen.how_it_works: Nitrogen in the atmosphere is chemically inert due to the strong triple bond between atoms. For plants to absorb it, nitrogen must be 'fixed' — converted into ammonia or nitrates. This is done by bacteria in the soil or industrial processes (e.g., the Haber-Bosch process). Like a safe with valuable contents: nitrogen is everywhere, but to use it, you need the right key.
key_problems: How to make the nitrogen fixation process more energy-efficient and environmentally friendly? How to reduce nitrogen oxide emissions from fuel combustion?
nitrogen.fun_fact: Liquid nitrogen at −196°C is used in cryotherapy to remove warts and in cooking for instant freezing of ice cream.

formulas.meaning: Nitrogen and hydrogen under pressure with a catalyst form ammonia

neon.name: Neon
neon.description: Neon (Ne) is an inert, colorless, odorless gas that glows bright red in an electrical discharge. Imagine a 'noble' gas that does not participate in chemical reactions but can spectacularly light up in advertising signs. In nature, neon is scarce — it makes up only 0.0018% of Earth's atmosphere.
neon.history: Neon was discovered in 1898 by Scottish chemist William Ramsay and English chemist Morris Travers. They isolated it from liquid air, noticing a bright red glow in a spectroscope. The name comes from the Greek 'neos' (new). Soon, neon was used in gas-discharge tubes, and by the 1910s, neon signs had conquered the world.
neon.how_it_works: When an electric current is passed through a tube containing neon, the gas atoms become excited, and upon returning to the ground state, they emit photons of characteristic red color. Different gases produce different colors: neon — red, argon — blue, helium — yellow. In everyday life, neon is used not only in advertising but also in lasers and as a cryogenic refrigerant.
key_problems: How to improve the efficiency of neon lamps compared to LEDs? What alternatives to neon in advertising signs are more environmentally friendly?
neon.fun_fact: Neon signs often actually contain argon with a phosphor — pure neon only gives a red color.

argon.name: Argon
argon.description: Argon (Ar) is an inert, colorless, odorless gas, the third most abundant in Earth's atmosphere (0.93%). It is like a 'silent guardian': it does not react with anything, so it is used to create a protective atmosphere in welding or for storing historical documents. In an electrical discharge, argon glows bluish.
argon.history: Argon was discovered in 1894 by Lord Rayleigh and William Ramsay. Rayleigh noticed that nitrogen from air was slightly heavier than nitrogen obtained chemically. This discrepancy led to the isolation of a new gas — argon (from Greek 'argos' — lazy, inactive). The discovery of argon initiated the study of noble gases.
argon.how_it_works: Argon is chemically inert because its outer electron shell is completely filled. Therefore, it does not form compounds (except exotic ones under extremely high pressures). In everyday life, argon is used in incandescent lamps to prevent oxidation of the tungsten filament, as well as in fire extinguishers and in the production of titanium.
key_problems: How to efficiently extract argon from air with lower energy costs? What new applications of argon are possible in medicine (e.g., neuroprotection)?
argon.fun_fact: Argon makes up about 1% of the Martian atmosphere, making it suitable for use as a shielding gas in future colonizations.

spiral_galaxy.name: Spiral Galaxy
spiral_galaxy.description_popular: This is a galaxy resembling a giant whirlpool with twisted arms of stars and gas.
spiral_galaxy.fun_fact_popular: Our Milky Way Galaxy is a spiral galaxy with two main arms and several smaller ones.
spiral_galaxy.description_simple: A spiral galaxy is a disk of stars, gas, and dust that rotates around a center, forming spiral arms. Imagine a fireworks display frozen in the shape of a rotating spiral. At the center there is usually a bright bulge, and sometimes a bar of stars.
spiral_galaxy.history_simple: In 1845, Lord Ross first noticed the spiral structure in the Whirlpool Galaxy. Edwin Hubble in 1926 classified spiral galaxies as a separate type.
spiral_galaxy.how_it_works_simple: The arms are not solid structures but regions of higher density where stars and gas accumulate, like traffic jams on a highway. Gravity and rotation create density waves that form the spiral pattern.
spiral_galaxy.description: A spiral galaxy is a type of galaxy characterized by a flat rotating disk with a central bulge and spiral arms. The arms contain many young hot stars, gas, and dust, giving them a bluish tint. Spiral galaxies are divided into ordinary (S) and barred (SB), where the bar is a strip of stars passing through the center. Examples: Milky Way (SBbc), Andromeda Galaxy (Sb), Whirlpool (Sc).
spiral_galaxy.history: The first observation of a spiral structure was made by William Parsons (Lord Rosse) in 1845. In 1926, Edwin Hubble developed the Hubble sequence, classifying galaxies by shape. Modern research using telescopes such as Hubble and JWST studies the evolution of spiral galaxies.
spiral_galaxy.how_it_works: Spiral arms form due to density waves that propagate through the galactic disk. Gravity compresses gas in the arms, triggering star formation. Young stars shine brightly, making the arms visible. Over time, stars leave the arms, but the density wave persists, creating the illusion of a rotating pattern.
key_problems: The mechanism of long-term stability of spiral arms is not fully understood.
The role of dark matter in the formation and maintenance of the spiral structure.
spiral_galaxy.fun_fact: Spiral galaxies make up about 60% of all galaxies in the nearby universe.

elliptical_galaxy.name: Elliptical Galaxy
elliptical_galaxy.description_popular: This is a galaxy shaped like an elongated ball, without spiral arms, resembling a giant cloud of old stars.
elliptical_galaxy.fun_fact_popular: Elliptical galaxies range from nearly round to highly elongated, like a football.
elliptical_galaxy.description_simple: An elliptical galaxy is a smooth, almost featureless cluster of stars with almost no gas and dust. Imagine a stadium filled with elderly people—there are few young people (new stars). Such galaxies usually contain many old red stars and very little interstellar matter.
elliptical_galaxy.history_simple: Edwin Hubble included elliptical galaxies in his classification in 1926. They are thought to form from the merger of spiral galaxies.
elliptical_galaxy.how_it_works_simple: Stars in an elliptical galaxy move chaotically, like bees in a swarm, rather than orderly in circles. Due to the lack of gas, new stars are almost never born, so the galaxy consists mainly of old stars.
elliptical_galaxy.description: Elliptical galaxies have shapes ranging from spherical to elongated ellipsoid, without spiral arms or bars. They contain little interstellar gas and dust, so star formation has almost ceased. Stars are mostly old, with low metallicity, and move on random orbits. Elliptical galaxies are divided into classes from E0 (nearly round) to E7 (highly elongated). They are often found in the centers of galaxy clusters.
elliptical_galaxy.history: Edwin Hubble introduced elliptical galaxies into his sequence in 1926. For a long time, they were thought to form from spirals after gas depletion. Modern models show they arise from mergers of spiral galaxies, confirmed by observations of merging systems.
elliptical_galaxy.how_it_works: Elliptical galaxies form through mergers of smaller galaxies, during which gas and dust are consumed in a starburst and then disappear. The remaining stars move chaotically, maintaining the elliptical shape through gravity. Without gas, new stars are not born, so the galaxy gradually dims. Dark matter plays an important role in holding the stars together.
key_problems: It is unknown why some elliptical galaxies rotate and others do not.
The role of black holes in the centers of elliptical galaxies is not fully understood.
elliptical_galaxy.fun_fact: The largest galaxies in the universe are elliptical, such as IC 1101, with a diameter of about 4 million light-years.

dwarf_galaxy.name: Dwarf Galaxy
dwarf_galaxy.description_popular: This is a small galaxy that contains only a few million or billion stars, unlike giants with trillions.
dwarf_galaxy.fun_fact_popular: Dwarf galaxies are the most common galaxies in the universe; there may be tens of times more of them than large ones.
dwarf_galaxy.description_simple: A dwarf galaxy is a small galaxy, ranging from a few hundred to a few thousand light-years in size. Imagine a village compared to a metropolis. They often orbit larger galaxies as satellites. Examples: the Magellanic Clouds, satellites of the Milky Way.
dwarf_galaxy.history_simple: The first dwarf galaxies were discovered in the early 20th century, but systematic study began only in the 1970s. Many dwarf galaxies are now known in the Local Group.
dwarf_galaxy.how_it_works_simple: Due to their low mass, dwarf galaxies easily lose gas when interacting with large galaxies, halting star formation. They can also be completely absorbed by larger neighbors.
dwarf_galaxy.description: Dwarf galaxies are small galaxies with low luminosity, containing from a few million to a few billion stars. They are subdivided into dwarf elliptical, dwarf spheroidal, dwarf irregular, and dwarf spiral. Dwarf galaxies are often satellites of larger galaxies. They play an important role in the study of dark matter, as their dynamics strongly depend on its presence.
dwarf_galaxy.history: The first dwarf galaxies (Magellanic Clouds) have been known since antiquity, but their nature as separate galaxies was established in the 20th century. In 1938, Harlow Shapley discovered the Sculptor Dwarf Galaxy. Modern surveys such as SDSS have detected many ultra-faint dwarf galaxies.
dwarf_galaxy.how_it_works: Dwarf galaxies are held together by gravity, with dark matter often dominating. Due to low gravity, they easily lose gas during tidal interactions or starbursts. Many are gradually disrupted and merge with larger galaxies. Their study helps understand the formation of structures in the universe.
key_problems: The 'missing satellites' problem: many more dwarf galaxies are predicted than observed.
The nature of dark matter in dwarf galaxies remains a mystery.
dwarf_galaxy.fun_fact: The dwarf galaxy Segue 2 contains only about 1000 stars—one of the smallest known galaxies.

protostar.name: Protostar
protostar.description_popular: This is a 'stellar embryo'—a cloud of gas and dust that contracts and heats up to become a real star.
protostar.fun_fact_popular: A protostar shines mainly in the infrared range because it is still too cold for visible light.
protostar.description_simple: A protostar is an early stage of star formation, when a huge cloud of gas and dust contracts under its own gravity. Imagine a snowball that becomes harder and warmer as you squeeze it. The temperature in the center of a protostar rises, but nuclear reactions have not yet begun.
protostar.history_simple: The idea of star formation from gas clouds was proposed in the 18th century by Immanuel Kant and Pierre-Simon Laplace. Modern observations of protostars became possible with the development of infrared astronomy in the 20th century.
protostar.how_it_works_simple: Gravity pulls gas toward the center, releasing energy that heats the protostar. When the core temperature reaches millions of degrees, thermonuclear fusion begins, and the protostar becomes a full-fledged star.
protostar.description: A protostar is a stage of stellar evolution following the collapse of a molecular cloud. It consists of a dense core surrounded by an accretion disk and a gas-dust envelope. The protostar shines due to gravitational energy released as matter falls in. Its temperature can reach several thousand kelvins, but nuclear reactions have not yet started. The protostar stage lasts from 100,000 to several million years depending on mass.
protostar.history: The concept of protostars follows from the Kant-Laplace nebular hypothesis (18th century). In the 20th century, the development of infrared astronomy allowed observations of protostars in dense clouds. Important contributions were made by Chandrasekhar and other astrophysicists on cloud collapse.
protostar.how_it_works: A molecular cloud contracts under gravity, fragmenting into clumps. At the center of a clump, a hydrostatic core—a protostar—forms. Matter continues to fall onto it through an accretion disk, releasing energy. When the core temperature reaches ~10^7 K, hydrogen fusion begins, and the protostar transitions to the main sequence star stage.
key_problems: The mechanisms of accretion termination and the onset of nuclear reactions are not fully understood.
The role of magnetic fields and rotation in protostar formation.
protostar.fun_fact: A protostar may be surrounded by a protoplanetary disk, from which planets subsequently form.

gas_giant.name: Gas Giant
gas_giant.description_popular: This is a huge planet consisting mainly of gas, with no solid surface, like Jupiter and Saturn in our solar system.
gas_giant.fun_fact_popular: Jupiter is so large that more than 1,300 Earths could fit inside it.
gas_giant.description_simple: A gas giant is a planet composed mainly of hydrogen and helium, with a small rocky core. Imagine a huge ball of gas that becomes denser toward the center. Such planets have no solid surface to land on. They also have powerful atmospheres and systems of rings and moons.
gas_giant.history_simple: Gas giants have been known since antiquity (Jupiter, Saturn). Their nature as planets without a solid surface was understood with the development of astronomy in the 17th–18th centuries.
gas_giant.how_it_works_simple: Gravity compresses gas in the center, creating high pressure and temperature. Hydrogen can transition to a metallic state, generating a magnetic field. The outer layers form bands and storms, like the Great Red Spot on Jupiter.
gas_giant.description: A gas giant is a giant planet composed predominantly of hydrogen and helium, with a mass greater than 10 Earth masses. It has no solid surface; below the atmosphere, the gas transitions to a supercritical fluid. In the interior, hydrogen under high pressure becomes metallic, generating a powerful magnetic field. Gas giants have many moons and rings. Examples: Jupiter, Saturn, and many exoplanets such as hot Jupiters.
gas_giant.history: Jupiter and Saturn have been known since antiquity. In 1610, Galileo discovered their moons. In the 20th century, the study of gas giants advanced with space probes (Voyagers, Galileo, Cassini). The discovery of exoplanets showed that gas giants are common in other star systems.
gas_giant.how_it_works: A gas giant forms from a protoplanetary disk, accreting gas onto a solid core. Internal pressure compresses hydrogen, turning it into liquid and then metallic. Convection in the atmosphere creates bands and vortices. The magnetic field is generated in the metallic hydrogen. The planet emits more heat than it receives from the star due to gravitational contraction.
key_problems: The exact composition and structure of gas giant cores are unknown.
The formation mechanism of hot Jupiters on close orbits remains unclear.
gas_giant.fun_fact: Saturn has the fastest winds in the solar system—speeds can reach 1800 km/h.

ice_giant.name: Ice Giant
ice_giant.description_popular: This is a planet composed mainly of water, methane, and ammonia in the form of ice and liquid, like Uranus and Neptune.
ice_giant.fun_fact_popular: Uranus and Neptune are called ice giants because they contain more 'ices' (water, methane, ammonia) than gases.
ice_giant.description_simple: An ice giant is a type of giant planet where the main components are water, methane, and ammonia, rather than hydrogen and helium. Imagine a planet where instead of a solid surface there is an ocean of liquid water under high pressure, and the atmosphere contains methane giving a blue color. Uranus and Neptune are examples in the solar system.
ice_giant.history_simple: Uranus was discovered in 1781 by William Herschel, Neptune in 1846 by mathematical calculations. In the 20th century, they were classified as ice giants.
ice_giant.how_it_works_simple: Below an atmosphere of hydrogen and helium lies a mantle of water, methane, and ammonia in the form of an ionic liquid. The pressure there is enormous, but not enough to make hydrogen metallic. This distinguishes them from gas giants.
ice_giant.description: An ice giant is a giant planet with a mass from 10 to 20 Earth masses, composed mainly of 'ices' (water, methane, ammonia) and having a thick atmosphere of hydrogen and helium. Unlike gas giants, they have no metallic hydrogen; their interiors consist of an ionic liquid. Uranus and Neptune are the only known ice giants in the solar system. They have a bluish color due to methane absorption of red light.
ice_giant.history: Uranus was discovered by William Herschel in 1781. Neptune was predicted by Urbain Le Verrier and discovered by Johann Galle in 1846. In the 1980s, Voyager 2 flew past both planets, providing detailed data. In the 2010s, they were classified as ice giants, distinct from gas giants.
ice_giant.how_it_works: An ice giant forms from a protoplanetary disk, accreting a significant amount of ices. Below the H2/He atmosphere is a mantle of water, methane, and ammonia in a supercritical state. The pressure in the mantle reaches several million atmospheres, but is insufficient for hydrogen metallization. Internal heat is released by gravitational contraction, but less than in gas giants.
key_problems: The internal structure of ice giants is poorly understood due to the lack of orbital missions.
The anomalous magnetic field of Uranus (tilted and offset) has no explanation.
ice_giant.fun_fact: Neptune has the strongest winds among the planets in the solar system—up to 2100 km/h.

superearth.name: Super-Earth
superearth.description_popular: This is a planet larger than Earth but smaller than Neptune, composed mainly of rock and metal.
superearth.fun_fact_popular: Super-Earths are the most common type of exoplanet discovered to date.
superearth.description_simple: A super-Earth is an exoplanet with a mass from 1 to 10 Earth masses, which may be rocky like Earth or have a thick atmosphere. Imagine Earth enlarged several times. Some super-Earths are in the habitable zone of their stars, making them candidates for the search for life.
superearth.history_simple: The first super-Earth was discovered in 1992 around a pulsar, but the first around a normal star (Gliese 876 d) was in 2005. Since then, thousands of such planets have been detected.
superearth.how_it_works_simple: Super-Earths form from a protoplanetary disk, accreting more material than Earth. Their composition can vary from rocky to water-rich. Due to their larger mass, they have stronger gravity and possibly a thicker atmosphere.
superearth.description: A super-Earth is a class of exoplanets with masses from 1 to 10 Earth masses and radii up to 2 Earth radii. They can be rocky like Earth or have a significant gas envelope. Super-Earths are often found in systems of red dwarfs. Their study is important for understanding the diversity of planets and conditions for life. Examples: Kepler-452b, TRAPPIST-1e.
superearth.history: The first super-Earth (around pulsar PSR B1257+12) was discovered in 1992. The first super-Earth around a normal star (Gliese 876 d) was in 2005. The Kepler mission (2009-2018) discovered thousands of super-Earths, showing they are a common type of planet.
superearth.how_it_works: Super-Earths form in a protoplanetary disk by accretion of solid material, and then may capture a gas envelope. Their internal structure depends on mass and composition: rocky planets have an iron core and silicate mantle; planets with a thick atmosphere may be mini-Neptunes. Surface gravity is higher than on Earth, affecting geology and climate.
key_problems: It is unknown which super-Earths are rocky and which are gaseous.
Conditions for life on super-Earths are unclear due to possible thick atmospheres.
superearth.fun_fact: Some super-Earths may be 'ocean planets' completely covered in water.

hot_jupiter.name: Hot Jupiter
hot_jupiter.description_popular: This is a planet the size of Jupiter, but located very close to its star, causing its atmosphere to be heated to thousands of degrees.
hot_jupiter.fun_fact_popular: The first hot Jupiter, 51 Pegasi b, was discovered in 1995 and launched the era of exoplanet discovery.
hot_jupiter.description_simple: A hot Jupiter is a gas giant that orbits its star at a very close distance, often less than 0.1 AU (for comparison, Mercury is 0.4 AU from the Sun). Due to its proximity to the star, its atmosphere is heated to 1000–3000 K. Such planets usually have elongated orbits and may be tidally locked.
hot_jupiter.history_simple: The discovery of 51 Pegasi b in 1995 by Michel Mayor and Didier Queloz was a sensation, as such a planet did not fit existing formation theories.
hot_jupiter.how_it_works_simple: Hot Jupiters likely form farther from the star and then migrate inward due to interactions with the protoplanetary disk or other planets. Proximity to the star causes strong heating, atmospheric expansion, and even evaporation.
hot_jupiter.description: A hot Jupiter is an exoplanet of the gas giant class with a mass on the order of Jupiter's mass, but with an orbital period of less than 10 days. Due to proximity to the star, their atmospheres are highly heated, leading to expansion and mass loss. They are often on tidally locked orbits, with one side always facing the star. Studying hot Jupiters provides information on planet migration and atmospheric evolution.
hot_jupiter.history: The first hot Jupiter, 51 Pegasi b, was discovered in 1995 by Michel Mayor and Didier Queloz (Nobel Prize 2019). This discovery prompted a revision of planet formation theories. Since then, hundreds of hot Jupiters have been detected, including HD 209458b, whose atmosphere was first measured.
hot_jupiter.how_it_works: Hot Jupiters form beyond the snow line, where there is plenty of ice, and then migrate inward due to gravitational interactions with the disk or other planets. Approaching the star, they experience strong heating, causing atmospheric expansion and mass loss. Some hot Jupiters may be destroyed by tidal forces.
key_problems: The migration mechanism of hot Jupiters (disk migration vs. gravitational scattering) is unclear.
Why do some systems have hot Jupiters and others not?
hot_jupiter.fun_fact: Some hot Jupiters have a 'tail' of evaporating gas, like a comet.

lhc.name: Large Hadron Collider
lhc.description_popular: The LHC is a huge underground particle accelerator that collides protons to find out what matter is made of.
lhc.fun_fact_popular: The LHC accelerates protons to 99.9999991% of the speed of light, making 11,245 revolutions per second.
lhc.description_simple: The Large Hadron Collider (LHC) is the world's most powerful particle accelerator, located in a 27-kilometer tunnel under the border of France and Switzerland. It collides beams of protons or ions at energies up to 13 TeV, creating conditions similar to those just after the Big Bang. Detectors (ATLAS, CMS, ALICE, LHCb) record the collision products, allowing the study of fundamental particles and forces.
lhc.history_simple: The idea of the LHC arose in the 1980s, construction began in 1998. The first beam was launched on September 10, 2008, but nine days later an accident occurred. Full operation began in 2010. In 2012, the Higgs boson was discovered, confirming the prediction of Peter Higgs and others.
lhc.how_it_works_simple: Protons are accelerated in a chain of accelerators (linear accelerator, booster, PS, SPS) and injected into the main LHC ring. Magnets keep particles on a circular trajectory, while radiofrequency cavities accelerate them. At intersection points, beams collide, and detectors record the particles produced.
lhc.description: The LHC is a proton-proton collider with a maximum collision energy of 14 TeV in the center-of-mass system. It is located in a 27 km tunnel at depths of 50 to 175 m. Beams consist of 2808 bunches, each containing about 1.15×10^11 protons. Luminosity reaches 2×10^34 cm^-2 s^-1. Main detectors: ATLAS and CMS (general purpose), ALICE (for quark-gluon plasma studies), and LHCb (b-quark physics). The LHC also accelerates lead ions for nuclear collision studies.
lhc.history: The concept of the LHC was proposed in 1984 at a conference in Lausanne. Construction was approved in 1994 and began in 1998. The first circulating beam was obtained on September 10, 2008, but on September 19 an accident occurred due to a faulty electrical connection, causing a helium leak. Repairs took 14 months. From 2010 to 2013, the LHC operated at 7–8 TeV, discovering the Higgs boson. After a shutdown for upgrade (LS1), operation resumed in 2015 at 13 TeV. In 2023, Run 3 began at 13.6 TeV. An upgrade to the High-Luminosity LHC is planned by 2029.
lhc.how_it_works: Protons from a hydrogen source pass through the linear accelerator LINAC2 (50 MeV), then the booster (1.4 GeV), PS (25 GeV), and SPS (450 GeV), after which they are injected into the main LHC ring. The ring has 1232 dipole magnets creating a field of 8.3 T to keep particles on track, and 392 quadrupole magnets to focus the beam. Acceleration is provided by 16 radiofrequency cavities (400 MHz). When protons collide, energy is released and converted into new particles according to E=mc^2. Detectors record tracks, energy, and momentum of particles, allowing their identification.
key_problems: The search for new physics beyond the Standard Model (supersymmetry, dark matter) has so far yielded no results.
Energy limitation due to ring size and magnetic field; a more powerful collider (FCC) is needed.
lhc.fun_fact: The LHC uses about 9600 magnets, including superconducting ones cooled with liquid helium to 1.9 K.

formulas.meaning: E is total energy, γ is the relativistic factor, m is rest mass, c is speed of light.

inflation.name: Inflation
inflation.description_popular: This is a brief period of super-fast expansion of the universe right after the Big Bang, when it increased in size by billions of billions of times in a tiny fraction of a second.
inflation.fun_fact_popular: Inflation explains why the universe is so homogeneous and flat, although it could have been curved and inhomogeneous initially.
inflation.description_simple: Inflation is a hypothetical stage of exponential expansion of the universe in the first 10^-35 seconds after the Big Bang. It was proposed to solve the horizon, flatness, and magnetic monopole problems. Inflation smoothed out inhomogeneities and created the primordial density fluctuations that later became galaxies.
inflation.history_simple: In 1981, Alan Guth proposed the inflationary model to explain why the universe looks so homogeneous. Later, Andrei Linde and others developed more realistic versions, such as chaotic inflation.
inflation.how_it_works_simple: Imagine blowing up a balloon: at first it is small and wrinkled, but then it suddenly inflates, becoming smooth and large. Inflation is such a rapid 'inflation' of the universe.
inflation.description: Inflation is a period of accelerated expansion of the universe, described by a scalar field (the inflaton). During inflation, the scale factor increased exponentially, smoothing out any initial inhomogeneities and curvatures. Quantum fluctuations of the inflaton generated the primordial density perturbations that became the seeds of large-scale structure. Inflation also predicts a specific spectrum of gravitational waves.
inflation.history: The idea of inflation was proposed by Alan Guth in 1981 to solve problems of the standard Big Bang model. However, his model had a 'graceful exit' problem. In 1982, Andrei Linde, as well as Andreas Albrecht and Paul Steinhardt, independently developed 'new inflation' models. Later, Linde proposed chaotic inflation, which became the most popular.
inflation.how_it_works: The inflaton is a field with high potential energy that slowly rolls down to a minimum. During rolling, the field causes repulsive gravity, leading to exponential expansion. When the field reaches the minimum, inflation ends, and the field's energy turns into particles (reheating). Quantum fluctuations of the field create inhomogeneities seen in the cosmic microwave background.
key_problems: The nature of the inflaton—which field is responsible for inflation—is unknown.
The problem of eternal inflation: in some models, inflation never ends globally, leading to a multiverse.
inflation.fun_fact: Inflation lasted only 10^-35 seconds, but during that time the universe expanded by a factor of 10^26—more than in all the subsequent 13.8 billion years.

electroweak_interaction.name: Electroweak Interaction
electroweak_interaction.description_popular: The electroweak interaction is the unification of two forces of nature: electromagnetism and the weak interaction, like the unification of electricity and magnetism.
electroweak_interaction.fun_fact_popular: At high energies, electromagnetism and the weak interaction become indistinguishable, like water at different temperatures.
electroweak_interaction.description_simple: The electroweak interaction is a single force that unifies the electromagnetic and weak interactions. It manifests at high energies, while at low energies it separates into two different interactions.
electroweak_interaction.history_simple: The theory of the electroweak interaction was developed by Sheldon Glashow, Abdus Salam, and Steven Weinberg in the 1960s.
electroweak_interaction.how_it_works_simple: At high energies, photons and W/Z bosons behave identically, but at low energies, the symmetry is broken and they become different particles.
electroweak_interaction.description: The electroweak interaction is a unified fundamental interaction that combines electromagnetism and the weak interaction. It is described by a gauge theory with the group SU(2)×U(1). The Higgs mechanism breaks the electroweak symmetry, giving mass to the W and Z bosons.
electroweak_interaction.history: The theory of the electroweak interaction was created in the 1960s by Glashow, Salam, and Weinberg. They predicted the existence of neutral currents (1973) and W/Z bosons (1983). Experimental confirmation earned them the Nobel Prize in 1979.
electroweak_interaction.how_it_works: At energies above 100 GeV, the electroweak symmetry is restored, and all gauge bosons are massless. At low energies, the Higgs mechanism breaks the symmetry, giving mass to the W and Z, while the photon remains massless. This explains the differences in the range of interactions.
key_problems: Hierarchy problem: why is the Higgs mass so small compared to the Planck mass?
electroweak_interaction.fun_fact: The prediction of W and Z bosons was confirmed in 1983, for which Glashow, Salam, and Weinberg received the Nobel Prize.

formulas.meaning: Kinetic terms for the W and B fields, describing the electroweak interaction.

strong_interaction.name: Strong Interaction
strong_interaction.description_popular: The strong interaction is the most powerful force in nature, gluing quarks together inside protons and neutrons.
strong_interaction.fun_fact_popular: The strong interaction is 100 times stronger than electromagnetism, but acts only over distances smaller than the size of an atomic nucleus.
strong_interaction.description_simple: The strong interaction is a fundamental force that holds quarks together inside hadrons (protons, neutrons) and binds protons and neutrons in the nucleus. Its carriers are gluons.
strong_interaction.history_simple: The concept of the strong interaction arose in the 1930s to explain nuclear forces. Quantum chromodynamics (QCD) was developed in the 1970s.
strong_interaction.how_it_works_simple: Quarks exchange gluons, creating a color field. The farther apart the quarks, the stronger the attraction, like a rubber band being stretched.
strong_interaction.description: The strong interaction is one of the four fundamental interactions, described by quantum chromodynamics (QCD). It acts between quarks and gluons, which carry color charge. The strong interaction is responsible for binding quarks into hadrons and nucleons into the nucleus.
strong_interaction.history: After the discovery of the neutron (1932), it became clear that a new force was needed to hold the nucleus together. Yukawa proposed the meson theory (1935). In the 1960s, the quark model and QCD were developed by Gell-Mann and others. Asymptotic freedom was discovered in 1973 by Gross, Politzer, and Wilczek.
strong_interaction.how_it_works: The strong interaction is mediated by gluons. Quarks have three colors (red, green, blue), gluons carry color-anticolor. The interaction exhibits confinement: quarks cannot be isolated. At high energies, the interaction weakens (asymptotic freedom).
key_problems: Quark confinement has not been proven analytically; the proton mass problem.
strong_interaction.fun_fact: Gluons themselves carry color charge, so they interact with each other, unlike photons.

formulas.meaning: The coupling constant α_s decreases with increasing momentum transfer Q.

weak_interaction.name: Weak Interaction
weak_interaction.description_popular: The weak interaction is the force responsible for radioactive decay, such as the transformation of a neutron into a proton.
weak_interaction.fun_fact_popular: The weak interaction is the only force that can change the type (flavor) of quarks, which is necessary for thermonuclear reactions in stars.
weak_interaction.description_simple: The weak interaction is one of the four fundamental forces, responsible for beta decay and other processes involving neutrinos. It acts over very short distances (10⁻¹⁸ m) and is mediated by W and Z bosons.
weak_interaction.history_simple: The weak interaction was first described by Fermi in 1934 to explain beta decay. The electroweak theory unified it with electromagnetism in the 1960s.
weak_interaction.how_it_works_simple: Particles exchange heavy W or Z bosons, leading to the transformation of one particle into another, for example, a neutron into a proton.
weak_interaction.description: The weak interaction is a fundamental interaction responsible for processes involving changes in quark and lepton flavor. It is mediated by massive W⁺, W⁻, and Z⁰ bosons. It has a small coupling constant and a very short range. It plays a key role in nuclear reactions in the Sun.
weak_interaction.history: In 1934, Fermi created the first theory of beta decay. In 1956, Wu experimentally discovered parity violation. In the 1960s, Glashow, Salam, and Weinberg unified the weak and electromagnetic interactions. W and Z bosons were discovered in 1983.
weak_interaction.how_it_works: The weak interaction occurs through the exchange of virtual W or Z bosons. For example, in beta decay, a neutron (udd) transforms into a proton (uud), emitting an electron and an antineutrino. The W boson carries charge, the Z boson mediates neutral currents.
key_problems: The origin of CP violation is not fully explained in the Standard Model.
weak_interaction.fun_fact: The weak interaction violates parity (P-symmetry) and CP-symmetry, which explains the predominance of matter over antimatter.

formulas.meaning: The amplitude is proportional to the square of the coupling constant and inversely proportional to the square of the transferred momentum minus the W mass.

planck_length.name: Planck Length
planck_length.description_popular: The Planck length is the smallest possible distance, about 1.6×10⁻³⁵ meters, below which the concept of length loses meaning.
planck_length.fun_fact_popular: If an atom were the size of Earth, the Planck length would be the size of a grain of sand.
planck_length.description_simple: The Planck length is a fundamental unit of length in quantum gravity, equal to √(ħG/c³). At such scales, quantum effects of gravity become important, and the classical description of spacetime breaks down.
planck_length.history_simple: The Planck length was introduced by Max Planck in 1899 as a combination of fundamental constants.
planck_length.how_it_works_simple: At distances on the order of the Planck length, gravity becomes quantum, and spacetime may be discrete, like pixels on a screen.
planck_length.description: The Planck length l_P = √(ħG/c³) ≈ 1.616×10⁻³⁵ m is the scale at which quantum effects of gravity become significant. It is believed that at this scale, spacetime ceases to be smooth and acquires a discrete or foamy structure.
planck_length.history: Max Planck in 1899 proposed a system of units based on fundamental constants: ħ, G, and c. The Planck length is one of them. In modern physics, it is considered the scale where a quantum theory of gravity is needed.
planck_length.how_it_works: The Planck length arises from the combination of constants when gravitational effects become comparable to quantum ones. For example, the de Broglie wavelength of a particle with Planck energy equals its gravitational radius. At smaller distances, quantum fluctuations of the metric become strong.
key_problems: Experimental achievement of Planck scales is impossible with current technology.
planck_length.fun_fact: The Planck length is 10²⁰ times smaller than the size of a proton.

formulas.meaning: The Planck length is expressed in terms of Planck's constant, the gravitational constant, and the speed of light.

planck_time.name: Planck Time
planck_time.description_popular: Planck time is the shortest possible interval of time, about 5.4×10⁻⁴⁴ seconds, below which time loses meaning.
planck_time.fun_fact_popular: In Planck time, light travels a distance equal to the Planck length.
planck_time.description_simple: Planck time is the time it takes for light to travel the Planck length. It is the smallest time interval that can be measured within known physics.
planck_time.history_simple: Like the Planck length, Planck time was introduced by Max Planck in 1899.
planck_time.how_it_works_simple: On times shorter than Planck time, quantum effects of gravity become important, and the concept of continuous time may be replaced by discrete 'quanta' of time.
planck_time.description: Planck time t_P = √(ħG/c⁵) ≈ 5.391×10⁻⁴⁴ s is a fundamental unit of time associated with the Planck length. It is believed that processes shorter than this time cannot be described by classical physics and require quantum gravity.
planck_time.history: Max Planck in 1899 defined Planck time as a natural unit of time. In modern cosmology, the Universe is thought to have originated at the Planck time after the Big Bang.
planck_time.how_it_works: Planck time is the time it takes for light to travel the Planck length. It is also the characteristic time for quantum gravity effects. For example, the age of the Universe in Planck units is about 8×10⁶⁰ t_P.
key_problems: What does the quantum structure of time look like at Planck scales?
planck_time.fun_fact: Planck time is 10⁴³ times smaller than the time it takes light to cross a proton.

formulas.meaning: Planck time is expressed in terms of Planck's constant, the gravitational constant, and the speed of light.

black_body.name: Black Body
black_body.description_popular: A black body is an ideal object that absorbs all incident radiation and re-emits it depending on its temperature.
black_body.fun_fact_popular: Stars, including the Sun, behave almost like black bodies, so their color depends on temperature.
black_body.description_simple: A black body is a physical model that absorbs all electromagnetic radiation of any frequency. When heated, it emits a continuous spectrum whose shape depends only on temperature.
black_body.history_simple: The concept of a black body was introduced by Gustav Kirchhoff in 1860. The problem of black body radiation led to the creation of quantum mechanics by Planck in 1900.
black_body.how_it_works_simple: A black body emits energy according to Planck's law: the higher the temperature, the more energy and the shorter the wavelength of the peak (like hot metal changing color from red to white).
black_body.description: A black body is an idealized body that completely absorbs all incident radiation of all frequencies. Its emissivity is described by Planck's law: the spectral energy density depends only on temperature. Real objects, such as stars, approximate a black body.
black_body.history: In 1860, Kirchhoff introduced the concept of an absolute black body. In 1896, Wilhelm Wien derived the displacement law. In 1900, Max Planck, trying to explain the black body spectrum, proposed energy quantization, which marked the beginning of quantum physics.
black_body.how_it_works: A black body emits electromagnetic waves due to the thermal motion of charged particles. The emission spectrum is continuous and described by Planck's formula. As temperature increases, the peak shifts to shorter wavelengths (Wien's law), and the total power increases as T⁴ (Stefan-Boltzmann law).
key_problems: An ideal black body does not exist, but good approximations are created in the laboratory.
black_body.fun_fact: Wien's displacement law states that the wavelength of the peak emission is inversely proportional to temperature.

formulas.meaning: Spectral radiance of a black body at frequency ν and temperature T.

hawking_radiation.name: Hawking Radiation
hawking_radiation.description_popular: Hawking radiation is a process by which black holes evaporate, emitting particles like a hot object cools down.
hawking_radiation.fun_fact_popular: Hawking radiation predicts that black holes are not eternal—they slowly lose mass and eventually disappear.
hawking_radiation.description_simple: Hawking radiation is a quantum effect in which black holes emit particles due to quantum vacuum fluctuations near the event horizon. This leads to a loss of black hole mass.
hawking_radiation.history_simple: Hawking radiation was predicted by Stephen Hawking in 1974 based on quantum field theory in curved spacetime.
hawking_radiation.how_it_works_simple: Near the event horizon, virtual particle-antiparticle pairs are created. One particle falls into the black hole, the other escapes, carrying away energy, which reduces the black hole's mass.
hawking_radiation.description: Hawking radiation is a predicted quantum effect in which black holes emit thermal radiation. It arises from quantum vacuum fluctuations in a strong gravitational field. The radiation spectrum is blackbody with a temperature inversely proportional to the black hole's mass.
hawking_radiation.history: Stephen Hawking in 1974 published a paper showing that black holes can radiate. This discovery united quantum mechanics, general relativity, and thermodynamics. Hawking radiation has not yet been observed experimentally due to its weakness.
hawking_radiation.how_it_works: In quantum field theory, the vacuum is not empty but contains virtual particle pairs. Near the event horizon, one particle may fall into the black hole, and the other escapes outward. The escaping particle has positive energy, while the infalling one has negative energy, reducing the black hole's mass. The process is analogous to evaporation.
key_problems: Hawking radiation has not been observed; the information loss problem in black hole evaporation.
hawking_radiation.fun_fact: Hawking radiation is extremely weak: for a black hole with the mass of the Sun, the temperature is only 60 nanokelvins.

formulas.meaning: The temperature of a black hole is inversely proportional to its mass M.

cosmological_constant.name: Cosmological Constant
cosmological_constant.description_popular: The cosmological constant is a mysterious force that causes the Universe to expand faster and faster, like an invisible engine.
cosmological_constant.fun_fact_popular: Einstein introduced it to make the Universe static, then called it his 'biggest blunder,' but it turned out to exist after all.
cosmological_constant.description_simple: The cosmological constant (Λ) is a parameter in the equations of general relativity that describes dark energy, causing the accelerated expansion of the Universe. It manifests as negative pressure that stretches space.
cosmological_constant.history_simple: Einstein introduced Λ in 1917 for a static Universe, but after the discovery of expansion (Hubble, 1929), he abandoned it. In 1998, the discovery of accelerated expansion revived Λ.
cosmological_constant.how_it_works_simple: The cosmological constant acts as antigravity: it repels galaxies from each other, causing the Universe to expand faster.
cosmological_constant.description: The cosmological constant Λ is a term in Einstein's equations representing the energy density of the vacuum. It leads to the accelerated expansion of the Universe. In modern cosmology, Λ is the main component of dark energy, responsible for the observed acceleration.
cosmological_constant.history: Einstein introduced Λ in 1917 for a static model of the Universe. After Hubble's discovery of expansion in 1929, he abandoned it. In 1998, observations of Type Ia supernovae showed that the expansion is accelerating, leading to the revival of Λ as a model for dark energy.
cosmological_constant.how_it_works: The cosmological constant enters Einstein's equations as an additional term: R_μν - 1/2 g_μν R + Λ g_μν = 8πG T_μν. It creates negative pressure p = -ρc², which counteracts gravity and accelerates expansion. Λ is time-independent and homogeneous.
key_problems: Why is the value of Λ so small (cosmological constant problem)?
Discrepancy between quantum field theory predictions and the observed value.
cosmological_constant.fun_fact: The value of the cosmological constant is extremely small (≈10⁻⁵² m⁻²), but its influence is noticeable on cosmological scales.

formulas.meaning: Equation of general relativity with cosmological constant Λ.

fermi_paradox.name: Fermi Paradox
fermi_paradox.description_popular: The Fermi paradox is the question: if there are many civilizations in the Universe, why don't we see them?
fermi_paradox.fun_fact_popular: Enrico Fermi asked this question over lunch in 1950, and there is still no definitive answer.
fermi_paradox.description_simple: The Fermi paradox is the contradiction between the high probability of the existence of extraterrestrial civilizations and the lack of observed evidence for their existence. The Universe should be full of life, but we see no traces.
fermi_paradox.history_simple: The paradox was formulated by physicist Enrico Fermi in 1950 during an informal conversation.
fermi_paradox.how_it_works_simple: If an advanced civilization existed, its traces (radio signals, probes) would be noticeable, but we have detected nothing, which makes us ponder the reasons for the silence of space.
fermi_paradox.description: The Fermi paradox is the apparent contradiction between high estimates of the number of intelligent civilizations in the galaxy (Drake equation) and the lack of any observed evidence of their existence. It raises questions about the prevalence of life, technological civilizations, and their longevity.
fermi_paradox.history: In 1950, Enrico Fermi during lunch at Los Alamos asked the question: 'Where is everybody?' Later, Frank Drake in 1961 derived an equation to estimate the number of civilizations. The paradox is actively discussed in astrobiology and SETI.
fermi_paradox.how_it_works: The paradox is based on estimates: the Milky Way has billions of stars, many with planets, the Universe is old. If at least one civilization began colonization, its traces would be noticeable. The absence of signals and artifacts leads to conclusions: either civilizations are rare, or short-lived, or avoid contact.
key_problems: Lack of observational data on extraterrestrial civilizations.
Unknown probability of the emergence of life and intelligence.
fermi_paradox.fun_fact: One possible explanation is the 'Great Filter': some catastrophe destroys civilizations before they become visible.

formulas.latex: N = R^* \cdot f_p \cdot n_e \cdot f_l \cdot f_i \cdot f_c \cdot L
formulas.meaning: The number of civilizations N in the galaxy with which contact is possible depends on seven factors.

copernican_principle.name: Copernican Principle
copernican_principle.description_popular: The Copernican principle states that Earth does not occupy a special position in the Universe, and we are not at the center of the cosmos.
copernican_principle.fun_fact_popular: This principle was revolutionary in the 16th century when Copernicus placed the Sun at the center, not the Earth.
copernican_principle.description_simple: The Copernican principle is a philosophical statement that Earth is not the center of the Universe and that observations from Earth are not privileged. In an extended sense: the Universe is homogeneous and isotropic on large scales.
copernican_principle.history_simple: The principle is named after Nicolaus Copernicus, who proposed the heliocentric system in 1543. In modern cosmology, it is used as the cosmological principle.
copernican_principle.how_it_works_simple: The Copernican principle means that the laws of physics are the same everywhere, and we are not in a special place, so our observations are typical for the entire Universe.
copernican_principle.description: The Copernican principle (or cosmological principle) states that the Universe is homogeneous and isotropic on large scales, and that Earth does not occupy a central or special position. This is a fundamental assumption of modern cosmology, underlying the Big Bang model.
copernican_principle.history: Nicolaus Copernicus in 1543 published 'On the Revolutions of the Heavenly Spheres,' moving Earth from the center. Later, Galileo, Kepler, and Newton developed this idea. In the 20th century, the principle was generalized to the cosmological principle, confirmed by observations of the cosmic microwave background.
copernican_principle.how_it_works: The Copernican principle allows extrapolating local laws of physics to the entire Universe. It justifies the use of homogeneous and isotropic models (e.g., the Friedmann-Lemaître-Robertson-Walker metric). Observations show that the distribution of galaxies and the cosmic microwave background are homogeneous on large scales.
key_problems: Are local deviations from homogeneity possible that violate the principle?
copernican_principle.fun_fact: The Copernican principle is often used as an argument against the anthropic principle: our existence does not mean the Universe was created for us.

formulas.latex: ds^2 = -c^2 dt^2 + a(t)^2 \left( \frac{dr^2}{1 - kr^2} + r^2 d\Omega^2 \right)
formulas.meaning: Metric of a homogeneous and isotropic Universe, based on the Copernican principle.

anthropic_principle.name: Anthropic Principle
anthropic_principle.description_popular: The anthropic principle says that we see the universe the way it is because only in such a universe could observers arise.
anthropic_principle.fun_fact_popular: This principle is often used to explain why physical constants have precisely the values they do, as if they are fine-tuned for us.
anthropic_principle.description_simple: The anthropic principle is a philosophical principle stating that the observed properties of the universe must be compatible with the existence of observers. The weak anthropic principle: we are located in a part of the universe where conditions are suitable for life. The strong anthropic principle: the universe must have properties that allow life.
anthropic_principle.history_simple: The term was introduced by Brandon Carter in 1973, although the idea dates back to ancient times.
anthropic_principle.how_it_works_simple: If physical constants were slightly different, life could not have emerged. Therefore, we observe precisely these constants because otherwise we would not exist.
anthropic_principle.description: The anthropic principle is a philosophical statement about the connection between the properties of the universe and the existence of observers within it. The weak anthropic principle: conditions in the observable universe must be compatible with the existence of life. The strong anthropic principle: the universe is obliged to have properties that allow life to develop.
anthropic_principle.history: The idea dates back to antiquity, but the modern formulation was given by Brandon Carter in 1973 at a symposium dedicated to the 500th anniversary of Copernicus. Carter distinguished between the weak and strong anthropic principles. Later, John Barrow and Frank Tipler developed this concept.
anthropic_principle.how_it_works: The anthropic principle is used to explain the 'fine-tuning' of physical constants. For example, if the gravitational constant were slightly larger, the universe would have collapsed; if smaller, stars would not have formed. The principle is not a physical theory but rather a methodological approach.
key_problems: Is the anthropic principle scientific? It is not falsifiable.
anthropic_principle.fun_fact: The anthropic principle is sometimes criticized as a tautology: 'We exist because we exist.'

vector_calculus.name: Vector Calculus
vector_calculus.description_popular: This is the mathematical language for describing flows, vortices, and fields, for example, how water flows in a river or how air swirls in a tornado.
vector_calculus.fun_fact_popular: Vector calculus helped engineers create the first computed tomography (CT) scan, which 'sees' the human body using the mathematics of fields.
vector_calculus.description_simple: Vector calculus is a branch of mathematics that studies vector fields, such as wind speed or Earth's magnetic field. It allows calculating how much 'substance' passes through a surface (flux) or how strongly a field swirls (curl). For example, it can be used to calculate how much water flows through a dam per second.
vector_calculus.history_simple: The foundations of vector calculus were laid by James Clerk Maxwell and Oliver Heaviside in the 19th century to describe electromagnetism. Maxwell used it for his famous equations, which unified electricity and magnetism.
vector_calculus.how_it_works_simple: Imagine you are standing in a wind field: at each point, the wind blows with a certain strength and direction. Vector calculus provides tools to measure how the wind 'diverges' from you (divergence) or how it 'swirls' around you (curl). It is like looking at a whirlpool: you can understand where water flows in and out.
vector_calculus.description: Vector calculus is a branch of mathematics that studies vector fields and scalar fields, and operations on them: gradient, divergence, curl, and Laplacian. It provides a language for formulating physical laws, such as Maxwell's equations in electrodynamics and the Navier-Stokes equations in fluid dynamics. The main theorems are the Gauss-Ostrogradsky theorem (relating the flux of a field through a closed surface to the divergence inside the volume) and Stokes' theorem (relating the circulation of a field around a contour to the curl through a surface). Vector calculus is indispensable in continuum physics, field theory, and differential geometry.
vector_calculus.history: Vector calculus developed in the 19th century thanks to the work of James Clerk Maxwell, who in the 1860s used quaternions to formulate electromagnetism. Later, Oliver Heaviside and Josiah Willard Gibbs simplified the notation, introducing modern vector operations. Maxwell, being one of the key scientists, laid the foundations, while Gibbs and Heaviside popularized vector calculus as an independent discipline.
vector_calculus.how_it_works: Vector calculus operates with vector fields F(x,y,z), where a vector is assigned to each point in space. The gradient of a scalar field f indicates the direction of greatest increase of f. Divergence ∇·F measures how much the field 'diverges' from a point (sources/sinks). Curl ∇×F measures the 'swirl' of the field. These operations are related by integral theorems: the flux through a closed surface equals the integral of divergence over the volume (Gauss' theorem), and the circulation around a closed contour equals the flux of curl through the surface (Stokes' theorem).
key_problems: The three-dimensional Navier-Stokes problem (existence and smoothness of solutions) is one of the Millennium Problems.
Development of efficient numerical methods for modeling turbulence.
vector_calculus.fun_fact: Without vector calculus, modern GPS systems would be impossible, as they use calculations of Earth's gravitational field.

formulas.meaning: The flux of a vector field F through a closed surface S equals the integral of the divergence of the field over the volume V bounded by that surface.
The circulation of a vector field F around a closed contour C equals the flux of the curl of the field through the surface S spanned by that contour.

differential_geometry.name: Differential Geometry
differential_geometry.description_popular: This is the science of curved surfaces and spaces, for example, how a sheet of paper bends when twisted, or how space curves around the Earth.
differential_geometry.fun_fact_popular: Differential geometry explains why light rays bend around massive objects — this is called gravitational lensing.
differential_geometry.description_simple: Differential geometry studies smooth curves and surfaces, as well as their generalizations — manifolds. It allows measuring curvature, distances, and angles on curved objects. For example, it can be used to calculate the shortest path between two points on the Earth's surface (a geodesic line).
differential_geometry.history_simple: The foundations were laid by Carl Friedrich Gauss in 1827 in his work 'General Investigations of Curved Surfaces'. Then Bernhard Riemann in 1854 generalized the ideas to multidimensional spaces, creating Riemannian geometry, which later became the mathematical foundation of Einstein's general theory of relativity.
differential_geometry.how_it_works_simple: Imagine you are crawling on the surface of an apple.
